name: Spring Boot Actuator Endpoints Exposed
description: Spring Boot Actuator exposes management endpoints (e.g., /actuator/env, /actuator/heapdump, /actuator/beans, /actuator/mappings) that can reveal environment variables, secrets, full class graphs, and live heap dumps when not properly secured. In many default configurations these endpoints are unauthenticated.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the running app for /actuator and its children (/actuator/env, /actuator/health, /actuator/heapdump, /actuator/mappings, /actuator/beans, /actuator/loggers, /actuator/trace, /actuator/httptrace). Enumerate both /actuator/* and legacy /management/* paths. Probe each without auth tokens.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 with JSON body containing keys like 'propertySources', 'contexts', 'beans', 'mappings', or a binary response (heapdump) from /actuator/heapdump. A 404 on /actuator but 200 on /actuator/health (partial exposure) is also flagged.

name: Actuator /env Leaks Credentials via Partial Masking Bypass
description: Even when /actuator/env masks secret values with '******', the property keys themselves (e.g., DB_PASSWORD, AWS_SECRET_KEY, STRIPE_SECRET) are still returned. Additionally, prior to Spring Boot 2.3, the /actuator/env/{name} single-property endpoint returns the raw unmasked value for some property sources.
runtime_discovery: GET /actuator/env without credentials. Then probe /actuator/env/{property-name} for individual entries (e.g., /actuator/env/spring.datasource.password). Compare key list against a dictionary of known secret property names.
detection_signal: Response body from /actuator/env contains property keys matching regex (password|secret|key|token|credential|api[-_]?key) even if values show '******'. A single-key probe /actuator/env/spring.datasource.password returning 200 with an unmasked value is a direct hit.

name: Broken Object-Level Authorization (IDOR) via Missing @PreAuthorize
description: Spring MVC REST endpoints that accept a resource ID in the path or query string (e.g., /api/orders/{id}) but rely only on URL-pattern security (HttpSecurity.antMatchers) without method-level @PreAuthorize checks allow any authenticated user to access any other user's resource by incrementing or fuzzing the ID.
runtime_discovery: Auth as user A, collect resource IDs from their normal responses. Auth as user B, substitute user A's IDs into identical requests. If user B receives user A's data, IDOR is confirmed. Also fuzz sequential/UUIDs in path and query parameters.
detection_signal: User B's request for /api/orders/{userA_id} returns HTTP 200 with user A's data rather than 403/404. Auth-differential comparison: same endpoint, same ID, different session tokens produces matching response bodies.

name: Mass Assignment via Spring Data / @RequestBody Binding
owasp_category: A08:2021 Software and Data Integrity Failures
description: When a Spring MVC controller uses @RequestBody to bind a JPA entity or DTO directly, an attacker can inject extra fields (e.g., 'role', 'isAdmin', 'accountBalance') into the JSON body that the application did not intend to be user-settable. Without explicit @JsonIgnoreProperties or a dedicated DTO, Spring's Jackson binding sets those fields.
runtime_discovery: Intercept a normal POST/PUT request body. Inject extra fields by appending 'role':'admin', 'isAdmin':true, 'enabled':true, 'balance':99999 to the JSON payload. Submit and observe whether the server reflects the injected values back in subsequent GET responses or login behavior changes.
detection_signal: A follow-up GET for the same resource returns the injected field value (e.g., 'role':'admin'). HTTP 200 on the mutating request with the injected field reflected in the response body. Elevation detected via auth-differential: re-auth after injection shows elevated permissions.

name: CSRF Protection Disabled in Spring Security Config
description: Spring Security enables CSRF protection by default, but many applications explicitly call http.csrf().disable() for REST API convenience. When the app also supports cookie-based sessions (as opposed to stateless JWT-only), this allows cross-site request forgery attacks against state-changing endpoints.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate via browser session. Check whether state-changing endpoints (POST /api/transfer, POST /api/settings) succeed without a CSRF token header or parameter. Observe Set-Cookie response header for session cookies lacking SameSite=Strict/Lax.
detection_signal: POST to a state-changing endpoint returns HTTP 200/201 when no X-XSRF-TOKEN or _csrf parameter is included, and the session cookie is present. Also: absence of XSRF-TOKEN cookie in Set-Cookie headers that would normally accompany Angular/Axios auto-CSRF.

name: H2 Database Console Exposed in Production
description: Spring Boot auto-configures the H2 in-memory database console at /h2-console when H2 is on the classpath and spring.h2.console.enabled=true. This web-based SQL console is often left enabled in staging/production, allowing unauthenticated full database access.
runtime_discovery: Probe /h2-console and /h2-console/login.jsp. Also check /console (alternate path). Attempt access without authentication.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response from /h2-console with HTML containing 'H2 Console' title or a login form with 'JDBC URL' and 'User Name' fields. Any successful connection to the console without prior app authentication is a critical finding.

name: Open Redirect via Spring Security Login Redirect Parameter
description: Spring Security's default login flow accepts a redirect_to or spring-security-redirect parameter (or a custom 'continue' / 'next' query param) after authentication. If not validated against an allowlist of internal paths, an attacker can craft a login URL that redirects users to an external phishing site post-authentication.
runtime_discovery: Append ?redirect_to=https://evil.com, ?spring-security-redirect=https://evil.com, or ?continue=//evil.com to the login URL. Authenticate successfully and observe the Location header in the 302 response.
detection_signal: HTTP 302 Location header after successful login points to an external or non-origin URL (e.g., https://evil.com or //evil.com). Any redirect leaving the application's origin after auth is flagged.

name: JWT 'none' Algorithm Accepted by Spring Security
description: Some Spring Security JWT configurations (especially custom or older libraries) accept tokens with the 'none' algorithm or allow algorithm confusion (RS256 → HS256 downgrade), letting an attacker forge arbitrary JWT claims including elevated roles without knowing the signing secret.
runtime_discovery: Capture a valid JWT from a normal login response. Modify the header alg to 'none' and strip the signature. Also try alg:'HS256' with the public key as the HMAC secret. Replay the modified token on protected endpoints.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 on a protected endpoint when the tampered token (alg:none, no signature) is sent in the Authorization header. Any successful authenticated response with a forged/unsigned JWT is a critical hit.

name: JPQL / HQL Injection via Spring Data @Query
description: When Spring Data repositories use @Query annotations with string concatenation (instead of named parameters) or when controllers pass user input directly into EntityManager.createQuery(), attackers can inject JPQL/HQL fragments to bypass WHERE clauses, extract additional entity properties, or cause errors that reveal the schema.
runtime_discovery: Inject JPQL metacharacters into search/filter form fields and query parameters: single quote ('), ' OR '1'='1, ' AND 1=2--, LIKE %'. Observe whether the response changes (returns extra records), throws a 500 with a Hibernate exception, or changes record count.
detection_signal: HTTP 500 response body containing 'org.hibernate.', 'QuerySyntaxException', 'antlr', or 'unexpected token' when a single quote or JPQL keyword is injected. Alternatively, injecting ' OR '1'='1 causes the endpoint to return all records instead of filtered results.

name: Overly Permissive CORS Configuration with allowCredentials
description: Spring MVC @CrossOrigin or WebMvcConfigurer CORS configurations sometimes set allowedOrigins('*') combined with allowCredentials(true), or use regex origin reflection (echoing back any Origin header) which creates a nearly identical risk. This allows any site to make credentialed cross-origin requests.
runtime_discovery: Send requests with arbitrary Origin headers (Origin: https://evil.com) to API endpoints. Inspect the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header — if it reflects back the attacker origin AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true is also present, the configuration is exploitable.
detection_signal: Response contains both 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com' (reflected attacker origin) and 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true'. Any combination of a non-null, non-same-origin ACAO with ACAC:true is flagged.

name: Spring Security / Whitelabel Error Page Leaks Stack Traces
description: Spring Boot's default Whitelabel error page and unhandled exceptions surface full Java stack traces, exception messages, and internal class/package names in HTTP responses when server.error.include-stacktrace is not set to 'never'. This reveals framework versions, internal paths, and dependency information useful for chaining attacks.
runtime_discovery: Trigger errors by sending malformed JSON bodies, unexpected content types (Content-Type: text/xml instead of application/json), invalid path parameters (e.g., /api/users/notanumber for a Long param), and oversized payloads. Observe the response body for stack traces.
detection_signal: HTTP 500 response body containing 'java.lang.', 'org.springframework.', 'at com.', or a JSON object with 'trace' key containing multiline stack frame strings. Also: HTML response with 'Whitelabel Error Page' and an exception message.

name: Actuator /loggers Endpoint Allows Log Level Manipulation
description: The /actuator/loggers endpoint accepts POST requests to change log levels at runtime. An attacker with access to this endpoint can enable TRACE/DEBUG logging for security-sensitive packages (e.g., org.springframework.security) to cause the app to log passwords, tokens, and session IDs to logs they may also be able to read via /actuator/logfile.
runtime_discovery: GET /actuator/loggers to confirm access. Then POST to /actuator/loggers/org.springframework.security with body {"configuredLevel":"TRACE"}. Attempt to read /actuator/logfile afterward. Also attempt POST to /actuator/loggers/ROOT to set all logging to DEBUG.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 on POST to /actuator/loggers/{name} without authentication. Subsequent /actuator/logfile or /actuator/httptrace responses containing authentication credentials, tokens, or sensitive request parameters in log lines.

name: Vertical Privilege Escalation via Missing Role Checks on Admin Endpoints
description: Spring Security URL matchers use first-match semantics and are case-sensitive on some configurations. Admin endpoints like /api/admin/users might be protected by .antMatchers('/api/admin/**').hasRole('ADMIN') but bypassed via path variations (/api/Admin/users, /api/admin/users/, /api/admin/users;x=y) or HTTP verb confusion (POST vs GET).
runtime_discovery: With a low-privilege authenticated session, enumerate admin-looking paths discovered from error messages, JS source, or /actuator/mappings. Try path case variations, trailing slashes, semicolon injection, and double slashes. Compare HTTP response codes and body content between admin and non-admin sessions.
detection_signal: Low-privilege session receives HTTP 200 with admin-level data on a path variant (e.g., /API/Admin/users or /api/admin/users/) that the canonical path would have returned 403 for. Auth-differential: same path variant returns different body for admin vs regular user.

name: Session Fixation — Session ID Not Rotated After Authentication
description: When Spring Security's session fixation protection is misconfigured (SessionCreationPolicy.NEVER or sessionFixation().none()), the session ID issued before login is reused after successful authentication. An attacker who pre-plants a known session cookie can hijack the session once the victim logs in.
runtime_discovery: Record the session cookie (JSESSIONID) value from a pre-authentication request. Authenticate successfully. Compare the session cookie value in the post-authentication response — if it is identical to the pre-auth value, session fixation protection is absent.
detection_signal: JSESSIONID (or equivalent Spring Session cookie) value is identical before and after a successful POST to /login. The Set-Cookie header in the login success response is absent (no new cookie issued) or contains the same token value.

name: Spring Data REST Auto-Exports All Repositories as Unauthenticated HATEOAS Endpoints
description: Spring Data REST auto-exports every JPA repository as a discoverable REST endpoint under /api or a configured base path. Without explicit @RepositoryRestResource(exported=false) or method-level security, all entities including internal ones (users, roles, audit logs) are readable and writable by any caller.
runtime_discovery: GET the HATEOAS discovery root (/ or /api) to enumerate all auto-exported collection endpoints from _links. Probe each collection without authentication. Attempt GET (list), POST (create), PATCH (modify), DELETE on each endpoint as an unauthenticated user and as a low-privilege user.
detection_signal: Unauthenticated GET to /api returns JSON with _links listing multiple entity collections. Fetching any collection link without a session returns HTTP 200 with entity data. A POST or PATCH returning 201/200 without authentication or for a resource the user should not own is a direct finding.

name: Express X-Powered-By Header Technology Disclosure
description: Express emits `X-Powered-By: Express` by default on every response. This header reveals the backend framework and its version range to attackers, narrowing the vulnerability search space for targeted exploitation. Many Node.js deployments also leak Node version via the `Server` header.
runtime_discovery: Crawl any endpoint and inspect response headers. Record the presence and value of `X-Powered-By`, `Server`, and `Via` headers across all observed responses to build a technology fingerprint.
detection_signal: Response header `X-Powered-By: Express` present on one or more URLs. Also watch for `Server: node` variants. Signal fires on any 2xx, 3xx, or 4xx response.

name: CORS Wildcard + Credentialed Request Misconfiguration
description: When `cors` middleware is configured with `origin: true` (reflect any origin) or an overly permissive regex, combined with `credentials: true`, any cross-origin site can make authenticated API calls on behalf of a logged-in user. This is distinct from a pure wildcard `*`, which browsers block for credentialed requests; the reflect-origin variant is the exploitable path.
runtime_discovery: Send OPTIONS preflight and GET/POST requests with an `Origin: https://attacker.example.com` header and a valid session cookie. Compare the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` response headers.
detection_signal: `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` echoes back the injected attacker origin AND `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` appears in the same response. Both headers present together on a credentialed request is the exploitable combination.

name: serve-static / express.static Directory Listing Enabled
description: When `express.static` or the `serve-static` middleware is configured with `{ dotfiles: 'allow' }` or `{ index: false }` without disabling directory listing, browsing to a directory URL returns an HTML listing of all files. This may expose source maps, `.env` backups, private keys, or build artifacts.
runtime_discovery: Crawl all static path prefixes (typically `/static`, `/public`, `/assets`, `/dist`). Send GET requests to directory-level URLs (trailing slash). Observe whether the response contains an HTML `<ul>` or `<table>` of file names.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response to a directory URL with `Content-Type: text/html` and body containing repeated `<a href=` file links or a visible folder listing pattern (e.g., `Index of /`).

name: JSON Body Prototype Pollution via body-parser
description: When an Express app accepts JSON bodies without sanitization, crafted payloads containing `__proto__`, `constructor`, or `prototype` keys can pollute the JavaScript Object prototype, causing downstream undefined-behavior bugs, authentication bypasses, or remote code execution depending on the sink.
runtime_discovery: Submit POST/PUT/PATCH requests to every JSON-accepting endpoint with a body containing `{"__proto__": {"isAdmin": true}}` and `{"constructor": {"prototype": {"polluted": "yes"}}}`. Then probe a subsequent GET endpoint to check if the injected key appears in server behavior (e.g., elevated privilege, changed response field).
detection_signal: Subsequent request to an unpolluted endpoint exhibits changed behavior (different role, 200 instead of 403, new field in response JSON) after the prototype-pollution payload was submitted. Alternatively, a 500 error with a Node.js stack trace containing `Cannot set property` on a null prototype chain.

name: Mass Assignment via Unsanitized req.body
owasp_category: A08:2021 Software and Data Integrity Failures
description: Express routes that pass `req.body` directly to ORM `create()` or `update()` calls allow attackers to set fields they should not control (e.g., `isAdmin`, `role`, `balance`). This is the Node.js/Mongoose equivalent of Rails' classic mass-assignment vulnerability.
runtime_discovery: Submit registration or profile-update forms with extra JSON fields beyond what the UI exposes (`isAdmin`, `role`, `accountBalance`, `verified`). Compare the resulting resource (via a subsequent GET) to see if the injected field was persisted.
detection_signal: A subsequent authenticated GET on the resource (user profile, product) returns the injected field value unchanged, or behavior changes (elevated access) consistent with the field being written. Auth-differential: User A submits `{"role":"admin"}`, then compare role-restricted endpoint responses between User A and a normal User B.

name: Missing or Weak Content-Security-Policy (helmet not applied)
description: Express apps that do not use `helmet` (or configure it with `contentSecurityPolicy: false`) send no `Content-Security-Policy` header, leaving users vulnerable to XSS by allowing inline scripts and arbitrary external sources. The absence of the header is observable from any response.
runtime_discovery: Inspect response headers from the application root and a sample of authenticated endpoints. Check for `Content-Security-Policy` and `X-Content-Type-Options` and `X-Frame-Options` headers.
detection_signal: `Content-Security-Policy` header absent from HTML responses. Bonus signals: also missing `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, or `Strict-Transport-Security`.

name: express-session Insecure Cookie Defaults (no Secure/HttpOnly/SameSite)
description: The `express-session` middleware defaults to `cookie: { secure: false, httpOnly: true, sameSite: undefined }`. In production over HTTP, the session cookie is transmitted in plaintext. Missing `SameSite=Strict/Lax` opens CSRF vectors. A hardcoded or weak `secret` (e.g., `'keyboard cat'`) enables session forgery.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate and inspect the `Set-Cookie` header for the session cookie. Check for `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, and `SameSite` attributes. Also compare the cookie name against known default values (`connect.sid`).
detection_signal: `Set-Cookie` header on the login response contains `connect.sid=...` without `Secure` and/or without `SameSite`. Alternatively, the cookie name is the unchanged default `connect.sid`, which fingerprints the exact middleware.

name: Path Traversal Through Static File Middleware
description: Improperly anchored `express.static` mounts or custom file-serving routes that concatenate user-supplied path segments without normalization allow directory traversal attacks (`../../etc/passwd`, `..%2F..%2F`). Encoded variants (`%2F`, `%5C` on Windows) may bypass naive string checks.
runtime_discovery: Fuzz static-file endpoint URL path segments with traversal sequences: `../`, `..%2F`, `..%5C`, `%2e%2e%2f`, `....//`. Target known static prefixes and any URL parameter that maps to a file path. Observe 200 responses and content-type changes.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response to a traversal URL with `Content-Type: text/plain` or `application/octet-stream` and body matching file content of a known server-side file (e.g., `root:` in `/etc/passwd`, or `package.json` contents). A 403 on normal paths but 200 on encoded traversal is also a signal.

name: Open Redirect in res.redirect() with User-Supplied URL
description: Express routes that call `res.redirect(req.query.next)` or `res.redirect(req.body.returnUrl)` without validation redirect users to arbitrary external URLs. This enables phishing, credential harvesting via fake login pages, and OAuth redirect-URI bypass chains.
runtime_discovery: Enumerate all redirect-triggering endpoints (login, logout, OAuth callbacks). Inject `?next=https://attacker.example.com`, `?redirect_uri=//evil.com`, and URL-encoded variants. Follow or inspect the `Location` header of 3xx responses.
detection_signal: HTTP 301/302/303 response with `Location` header containing the injected attacker domain verbatim (e.g., `https://attacker.example.com`). Scanner confirms the reflected URL is not a same-site relative path.

name: JWT Algorithm Confusion / 'alg:none' Acceptance
description: Libraries such as `jsonwebtoken` prior to fixes (and misconfigured wrappers) that do not pin the accepted algorithm to `RS256`/`HS256` may accept tokens with `alg: none` (unsigned) or allow HS256 when the server expects RS256, enabling an attacker to forge arbitrary claims including admin roles.
runtime_discovery: Obtain a valid JWT from the login endpoint. Craft a new token with the header `{"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}`, sign with an empty string, and send it in the `Authorization` header. Also try switching from RS256 to HS256 signed with the app's public key as the HMAC secret.
detection_signal: A 200 response (instead of 401/403) when sending a forged `alg:none` token or an HS256 token signed with the known public key. Auth-differential: the forged token grants the same access level as a legitimately issued token.

name: NoSQL Injection via MongoDB Operator Injection
description: Express apps that pass JSON request body or query parameters directly to Mongoose/MongoDB find queries are vulnerable to operator injection. Replacing a string value with `{"$gt": ""}` or `{"$ne": null}` changes the query semantics, enabling authentication bypass, data extraction, and denial of service.
runtime_discovery: For login and search endpoints, replace string fields with JSON operator objects: send `{"username": {"$gt": ""}, "password": {"$gt": ""}}` as the request body. Also test query-string form: `?filter[$ne]=x`. Observe authentication success or unexpected data returned.
detection_signal: Login returns a valid session/token (HTTP 200 with auth cookie) when credentials are replaced with `{"$gt": ""}`, or a search endpoint returns more records than expected with operator-injected parameters. A 500 with a MongoDB error message is also a positive signal revealing the injection point.

name: Debug / Admin Routes Exposed Without Authentication
description: Express apps commonly ship debug routes (`/metrics`, `/__health`, `/_debug`, `/admin`, `/api-docs`, `/swagger`, `/graphql`) that expose environment variables, heap snapshots, Prometheus metrics, or full API schemas. Without authentication middleware these are accessible to anyone.
runtime_discovery: Enumerate a wordlist of common Express debug/admin path fragments (`/metrics`, `/health`, `/healthz`, `/__status`, `/admin`, `/graphql`, `/api-docs`, `/swagger.json`, `/openapi.json`, `/env`) as unauthenticated requests. Record 200 responses and analyze response bodies.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response to an unauthenticated request to a sensitive path containing Prometheus metric lines (`# HELP`, `# TYPE`), JSON with `process.env`, an OpenAPI schema object (`{"openapi":"3.`), or a GraphQL introspection result (`__schema`).

name: Insecure Direct Object Reference via Sequential / Guessable IDs
description: Express REST APIs that expose resources at `/users/:id`, `/orders/:id`, or `/files/:id` with sequential or short numeric IDs allow horizontal privilege escalation when the route handler does not verify that the authenticated user owns the requested ID.
runtime_discovery: Auth-differential: Authenticate as User A and record their resource ID (e.g., `/api/users/1042`). Authenticate as User B and attempt to GET/PUT/DELETE User A's resource ID using User B's session token. Compare HTTP status codes and response body contents.
detection_signal: User B's authenticated request to User A's resource returns HTTP 200 with User A's data (not a 403). Alternatively, incrementing the numeric ID in a URL returns different users' data with the same session. Response body differs between User A and User B's own resource but is accessible to both.

name: Rate Limiting Absent or Bypassable on Sensitive Endpoints
description: Express apps that omit `express-rate-limit` (or similar) on authentication, password-reset, and OTP endpoints allow unlimited brute-force attempts. Even when rate-limiting is applied, misconfigured `keyGenerator` functions using `X-Forwarded-For` as the key allow trivial bypass by spoofing that header.
runtime_discovery: Send 50-200 rapid POST requests to `/login`, `/forgot-password`, and `/verify-otp` endpoints. Also send the same burst with varying `X-Forwarded-For: 1.2.3.x` headers to test key-generator bypass. Measure response status codes and time-to-first-429.
detection_signal: No HTTP 429 after 50+ rapid identical login attempts, or: a 429 is triggered on the real IP but disappears when `X-Forwarded-For` is rotated. Timing uniformity (no delay increase) across 100 requests also confirms absent rate limiting.

name: Server-Side Request Forgery via User-Controlled URL in axios / got
description: Express routes that accept a URL parameter and pass it to `axios.get(req.query.url)` or `fetch(req.body.endpoint)` without allowlist validation allow SSRF. Attackers can reach internal metadata services (169.254.169.254), Redis, internal APIs, and localhost services not exposed to the internet.
runtime_discovery: Identify endpoints that fetch external content (webhook testers, URL preview/screenshot features, import-from-URL flows). Inject internal targets: `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/`, `http://localhost:6379/`, `http://127.0.0.1:8080/admin`, and a known collaborator URL to confirm out-of-band requests.
detection_signal: Response body contains AWS/GCP/Azure cloud metadata content (e.g., `ami-id`, `instance-id`, `iam/security-credentials`). Alternatively, a collaborator server receives an inbound HTTP request from the app's IP after the probe. Or a 200 response with Redis inline protocol text (`+OK`, `-ERR`).

name: Next.js API Route Missing HTTP Method Guard
description: Next.js API route handlers (`pages/api/*.js` or `app/api/*/route.ts`) that do not check `req.method` accept every HTTP verb. A DELETE or PUT sent where only GET was intended can trigger unintended mutations, data deletion, or privilege escalation without any authentication bypass.
runtime_discovery: For each discovered API endpoint (crawled from HTML, JS bundles, or sitemap), replay the request with all HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS). Compare status codes and response bodies across verbs. A 200 or 201 on a destructive verb that should return 405 is a confirmed finding.
detection_signal: HTTP 200/201/204 on DELETE, PUT, or PATCH to an endpoint where only GET/POST was observed in normal app traffic; response body indicates state change (e.g., `{"deleted":true}`, `{"updated":true}`).

name: Next.js API Route Unauthenticated Access (Missing Session Check)
description: API routes under `pages/api/` or `app/api/` that forget to validate the session/JWT before reading or writing data are fully accessible anonymously. This is extremely common in Next.js apps where auth is handled ad-hoc per route rather than via middleware.
runtime_discovery: Replay every API request observed during an authenticated crawl session, but strip the session cookie / Authorization header. Compare response: if the unauthenticated replay returns the same 200 with data as the authenticated version, the route is unprotected. Enumerate additional routes by fuzzing common Next.js patterns (`/api/user`, `/api/me`, `/api/admin/users`, `/api/dashboard`).
detection_signal: HTTP 200 with non-empty JSON body on an API route when the Authorization / Cookie header is absent or replaced with an invalid token; auth-diff between user-A session and no-session returns identical payload.

name: Next.js Middleware Auth Bypass via `x-middleware-subrequest` Header (CVE-2025-29927)
description: Next.js versions before 15.2.3 / 14.2.25 trust the internal `x-middleware-subrequest` header to skip middleware re-execution. An external attacker who sends this header can bypass all `middleware.ts` auth/redirect logic — including JWT validation and role checks — and reach protected routes directly.
runtime_discovery: For every route that redirects unauthenticated requests (observed as 302/307 during an anonymous crawl), replay the request adding the header `x-middleware-subrequest: middleware` (or `pages/_middleware`). If the response changes from a redirect to a 200 with protected content, the middleware is bypassed.
detection_signal: Route previously returning HTTP 302/307 redirect to `/login` now returns HTTP 200 with protected page content when `x-middleware-subrequest: middleware` header is present; `x-powered-by: Next.js` header confirms Next.js stack.

name: Next.js Server Action IDOR / Missing Ownership Check
description: Next.js Server Actions (App Router `'use server'` functions) are exposed as POST endpoints to `/__nextjs_original-stack-frames` or as bound action IDs. When the action accepts a resource ID from the request body without verifying ownership, any authenticated user can manipulate another user's data by replaying the action with a different ID.
runtime_discovery: Intercept form submissions that trigger server actions (identified by `Next-Action` request header or form `action` attribute pointing to the same URL with a POST). Replay the same POST as a second authenticated user (auth-diff), or enumerate adjacent integer/UUID IDs in the action payload. Compare responses for data leakage or modification success.
detection_signal: POST to page URL with `Next-Action: <action-id>` header; substituting a resource ID belonging to another user returns HTTP 200 with that user's data or a success confirmation instead of a 403/404.

name: Next.js RSC Sensitive Props Leaked to Client Bundle
description: React Server Components that pass sensitive data (API keys, user PII, internal config) as props to Client Components cause that data to be serialized into the RSC payload and embedded in the HTML or a `__NEXT_DATA__` script tag, making it visible to any browser user.
runtime_discovery: Crawl pages and inspect inline `<script id='__NEXT_DATA__'>` JSON and `__RSC_MANIFEST__` blocks in the HTML source. Also fetch `/_next/static/chunks/*.js` and search for high-entropy strings, email patterns, internal URLs, or secret-shaped tokens. No authentication needed for publicly accessible pages.
detection_signal: Regex match for API key patterns (e.g., `sk-`, `AKIA`, `ghp_`), internal hostnames, or PII inside `__NEXT_DATA__` JSON blob or `_next/static` JS chunks; or RSC flight payload (`text/x-component`) containing credential-shaped strings.

name: Next.js Open Redirect in API Route or `next.config.js` Redirects
description: API routes that call `res.redirect(req.query.returnUrl)` without validating the destination, or `next.config.js` redirect rules with insufficiently anchored `source` patterns, can be abused to redirect users to attacker-controlled domains for phishing.
runtime_discovery: Enumerate query parameters named `redirect`, `return`, `returnUrl`, `next`, `url`, `continue`, `goto` across all API routes and pages. Inject `https://evil.com` and observe the Location header in the response. Also fuzz the `destination` parameter on any observed `/api/auth/*` (e.g., NextAuth callback URLs).
detection_signal: HTTP 302/307/308 response with `Location: https://evil.com` (or any external domain not in the app's allowlist) after injecting an external URL into a redirect-shaped query parameter.

name: Next.js SSR/API Route SSRF via User-Controlled URL
description: SSR pages and API routes that use `fetch()`, `axios`, or `node:http` with a URL derived from query parameters or request body without allowlist validation can be coerced into making requests to internal services (metadata endpoints, Redis, Postgres, internal dashboards).
runtime_discovery: Identify parameters that appear to control upstream URLs (e.g., `?url=`, `?src=`, `?endpoint=`, `?host=`, `?proxy=`). Inject `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (AWS IMDSv1), `http://localhost:6379` (Redis), and `http://169.254.169.254/computeMetadata/v1/` (GCP). Measure response time difference vs. a known-unreachable host for blind SSRF. Observe response body for internal data.
detection_signal: Response body contains AWS instance metadata structure, Redis RESP protocol bytes, or internal JSON not matching public API contracts; OR >2 s response time delta compared to baseline when injecting non-routable IPs (blind SSRF timing signal).

name: Next.js API Route Overly Permissive CORS
description: API routes that set `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` or reflect the `Origin` header unconditionally (including `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`) allow cross-site requests from any origin, enabling cross-origin data theft when combined with session cookies.
runtime_discovery: Send an OPTIONS preflight and a credentialed cross-origin GET/POST to each API route with `Origin: https://evil.example.com`. Inspect response headers. If `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` echoes back the injected origin AND `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` is present, the route is exploitable for cross-origin data theft.
detection_signal: `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com` mirrors back the injected Origin header AND `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` is set in the same response.

name: Next.js Path Traversal in Custom Static File / Image Serving Routes
description: Custom API routes that serve files from disk using `req.query.file` or `req.query.path` combined with `fs.readFile` without path normalization can be exploited with `../` sequences to read arbitrary server-side files such as `.env`, `next.config.js`, or `package.json`.
runtime_discovery: Enumerate API routes or Next.js Image Optimization proxy endpoints that accept a filename or path query parameter. Inject `../../../../etc/passwd`, `..%2F..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd`, and `....//....//etc/passwd`. Also try `../../../.env` and `../../../next.config.js`. Compare response content to baseline.
detection_signal: Response body contains Unix `/etc/passwd` format lines, `.env` `KEY=VALUE` pairs, or `next.config.js` JavaScript source; HTTP 200 with `Content-Type: text/plain` or `application/octet-stream` for a path-parameter-driven endpoint.

name: Next.js `NEXT_PUBLIC_` Secret Leakage in Client Bundle
description: Environment variables prefixed with `NEXT_PUBLIC_` are inlined verbatim into the client-side JavaScript bundle at build time. Developers who mistakenly prefix secret keys (Stripe, AWS, database DSNs) with `NEXT_PUBLIC_` expose them to any browser visitor.
runtime_discovery: Fetch all `/_next/static/chunks/*.js` files discovered from the app's HTML. Apply regex patterns for known secret formats (Stripe `sk_live_`, AWS `AKIA`, SendGrid `SG.`, etc.) and also search for the literal string `NEXT_PUBLIC_`. No authentication needed.
detection_signal: String match for `NEXT_PUBLIC_` followed by a secret-shaped value (high-entropy string, API key prefix) inside `/_next/static/chunks/` JavaScript files; or direct match for known secret prefixes (`sk_live_`, `AKIA`, `SG.`) in bundle source.

name: Next.js Error Overlay / Stack Trace Exposure in Production
description: Next.js apps misconfigured with `NODE_ENV=development` in production, or with custom error pages that re-render the full Next.js error overlay, leak full stack traces, file system paths, environment variable names, and source code snippets to end users.
runtime_discovery: Trigger server errors by sending malformed JSON bodies, invalid query parameter types, or non-existent route parameters to SSR pages and API routes. Observe the HTML response body for stack trace patterns (`Error:`, `at Object.<anonymous>`, file system paths `/home/`, `/app/`, `node_modules/`). Also check the `x-nextjs-cache` and `x-powered-by` headers for version disclosure.
detection_signal: HTTP 500 response body contains a JavaScript stack trace string with absolute file paths (e.g., `/app/pages/api/`, `/home/node/`) or the Next.js development error overlay HTML (`__NEXT_ERROR__`, `_next/static/development/`); or `x-powered-by: Next.js` combined with version number in error body.

name: Next.js Server Action Cross-Site Request Forgery
description: Next.js Server Actions before version 14.1.1 lacked proper origin checking. Apps on older versions (or those that disabled the built-in CSRF protection by misusing the `headers()` API) can be triggered cross-site. A malicious page can craft a form that POSTs to a victim's Next.js app and invokes state-mutating server actions using the victim's session cookie.
runtime_discovery: Identify server action endpoints (POST to page URL with `Next-Action` header). Replay the POST from a different origin by setting `Origin: https://evil.example.com` and omitting the Referer header. If the action executes successfully (HTTP 200 with mutation result rather than 403), CSRF protection is absent or bypassed.
detection_signal: Server action POST with `Origin: https://evil.example.com` returns HTTP 200 with action result JSON (not a CSRF rejection); response body matches expected mutation output (e.g., `{"status":"ok"}`); `Next-Action` header is accepted cross-origin.

name: Next.js / Node.js Prototype Pollution via Query String Parsing
description: Next.js API routes using `qs` or `body-parser` with default settings, or older versions of Next.js itself, can be vulnerable to prototype pollution through query parameters like `?__proto__[admin]=true` or `?constructor.prototype.isAdmin=true`. This can bypass authorization checks that read properties from plain objects.
runtime_discovery: Inject prototype-polluting payloads into query strings and JSON request bodies: `?__proto__[isAdmin]=true`, `?constructor[prototype][role]=admin`, and `{"__proto__":{"isAdmin":true}}` in POST bodies. Then issue a follow-up request that exercises the affected auth check. Observe if the response indicates elevated privilege or different behavior.
detection_signal: Follow-up request after prototype-pollution probe returns a response body or HTTP status (200 vs 403) consistent with elevated privilege; or the app returns a 500 error with a stack trace referencing `Object.prototype` when the pollution payload causes a type error downstream.

name: Next.js i18n Locale Prefix Middleware Auth Bypass
description: When `next.config.js` defines `i18n` locales and middleware guards specific path prefixes (e.g., `/dashboard`), accessing the same route with a locale prefix (e.g., `/en/dashboard`, `/fr/dashboard`) may bypass the middleware matcher if the matcher pattern is not locale-aware, exposing protected SSR pages without authentication.
runtime_discovery: For every path that redirects unauthenticated requests to login (observed as 302 during anonymous crawl), prefix it with each configured locale slug (`/en/`, `/fr/`, `/de/`) and any arbitrary locale slug. Compare responses. If the locale-prefixed path returns 200 with protected content instead of a redirect, middleware matching is incomplete.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response with protected page HTML content at `/en/<protected-path>` where `/protected-path>` returns HTTP 302; locale-prefixed URL bypasses session check; `set-cookie` header is absent on the 200 response confirming no auth was enforced.

name: Next.js `rewrites()` Exposing Internal / Admin API Routes
description: The `rewrites()` function in `next.config.js` can map public-facing paths to internal API routes intended to be backend-only. If an internal route like `/api/internal/admin` is rewritten from `/api/v2/users` without auth, or if the rewrite configuration accidentally makes internal routes reachable under a predictable public alias, attackers can call admin endpoints directly.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the app and enumerate all observed API route patterns. Fuzz common admin/internal path aliases (`/api/internal/*`, `/api/admin/*`, `/_internal/*`, `/api/v1/admin/*`). Also probe `/_next/` subpaths beyond static assets. Look for mismatched access control between the canonical route and any rewrite aliases discovered.
detection_signal: A fuzzed path returns HTTP 200 with admin-shaped JSON (user lists, config data, internal metrics) where the canonical protected route would return 401/403; or the response body is identical to a known admin endpoint but accessible without an admin session cookie.

name: REACT_APP_* / VITE_ Secrets Baked Into JS Bundle
description: Build tools that prefix environment variables (REACT_APP_*, VITE_*, NEXT_PUBLIC_*) inline them verbatim into the client-side JS bundle at build time. API keys, private endpoint URLs, and third-party service secrets end up shipped to every browser that loads the app.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the app and collect all .js chunk URLs from the HTML. Fetch each chunk and scan the response body for high-entropy strings, known key prefixes (sk_, AIza, AKIA, ghp_, etc.), and well-known variable name patterns like REACT_APP_ or VITE_. No authentication required.
detection_signal: Regex match of known secret prefixes (sk_live_, AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}, AIzaSy, ghp_) or high-entropy Base64/hex strings of length 20+ inside a text/javascript response body fetched from a public chunk URL.

name: Client-Side-Only Authorization (Hidden Route / Conditional Render Bypass)
description: React apps frequently hide admin routes or premium features by conditionally rendering components or redirecting in the client router, with no corresponding server-side enforcement. An attacker navigates directly to the hidden route URL or manually calls the backing API.
runtime_discovery: Parse the JS bundle for React Router route definitions (Route, createBrowserRouter path values) and collect every path string. Attempt direct navigation to each path as an unauthenticated or low-privilege user. Observe whether the app renders protected content or whether the API calls succeed when made directly.
detection_signal: A route path extracted from the bundle (e.g. /admin, /billing, /internal) returns HTTP 200 with meaningful HTML/JSON content when accessed directly by a non-admin session, rather than redirecting (302) or returning 401/403.

name: Redux / Zustand Store Exposed in Window or DevTools
description: Production builds that leave Redux DevTools middleware active, or that attach the store to window.__REDUX_STORE__ / window.__ZUSTAND__, expose the full application state — including auth tokens, PII, and session data — to any script running in the page context.
runtime_discovery: After logging in, inject a short script via browser console (or via the scanner's injected content script) to read window.__REDUX_DEVTOOLS_EXTENSION__ presence, window.__STORE__, or window.__ZUSTAND__. Serialize and inspect the state tree for sensitive keys (token, accessToken, ssn, dob, email).
detection_signal: window.__REDUX_DEVTOOLS_EXTENSION__ is truthy in a production build, OR window[storeKey] resolves to an object with a getState() method that returns fields matching secret/token/credential patterns.

name: Overly Permissive CORS on the Backing REST/GraphQL API
description: The React SPA's backing API returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * or reflects the request Origin unconditionally, combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, allowing any origin to make authenticated cross-site requests on behalf of a logged-in user.
runtime_discovery: Send a preflight OPTIONS (and then a credentialed GET/POST) to each discovered API endpoint with an arbitrary Origin: https://evil.example.com header. Inspect the response headers for the reflected or wildcard ACAO value and whether ACAC: true is present simultaneously.
detection_signal: Response contains Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com (reflected) together with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, or ACAO: * with credentials mode allowing reads of sensitive data.

name: JWT / Bearer Token Stored in localStorage (XSS-Accessible)
description: React apps commonly store JWTs in localStorage or sessionStorage for convenience. Unlike HttpOnly cookies, these stores are fully readable by JavaScript, making any XSS vulnerability an instant full-account-takeover: the token can be exfiltrated in a single fetch call.
runtime_discovery: After authenticating, read localStorage and sessionStorage keys via injected script. Match key names containing 'token', 'jwt', 'auth', 'access', 'bearer'. Decode the Base64url payload to confirm it is a JWT. Also check for tokens stored in non-HttpOnly cookies.
detection_signal: localStorage.getItem(key) or document.cookie returns a value matching the JWT three-part Base64url pattern (eyJ[A-Za-z0-9_-]+\.eyJ[A-Za-z0-9_-]+\.[A-Za-z0-9_-]+), confirmed by a decodable header with alg field.

name: dangerouslySetInnerHTML / innerHTML Sink Leading to Stored/Reflected XSS
description: When React components pass user-controlled data into dangerouslySetInnerHTML, or downstream DOM manipulation (via refs, jQuery, or third-party rich-text editors) bypasses React's escaping, attacker-supplied HTML/JS executes in other users' browsers.
runtime_discovery: Fill text inputs, URL parameters, and profile fields with an XSS canary payload (e.g. ). Submit and then navigate to any page that would render that content. Check whether window.__xss is set, or intercept network requests for an out-of-band callback.
detection_signal: Injected canary string appears unescaped in a subsequent page's DOM (innerHTML contains literal <script> or event handler attributes), OR the onerror/onload callback fires as confirmed by window property set or out-of-band DNS/HTTP ping.

name: Open Redirect via SPA Router ?redirect= / returnTo= Parameter
description: Login and OAuth callback pages in React apps commonly accept a ?redirect= or ?returnTo= query parameter and navigate() there after authentication without validating the host, enabling phishing redirects to attacker-controlled domains.
runtime_discovery: Identify login or callback URLs by crawling. Append ?redirect=https://evil.example.com (and variants: //evil.example.com, /\evil.example.com, %2F%2Fevil.example.com). Trigger the login flow and observe the final navigation target after authentication.
detection_signal: After the authentication flow completes, the browser location ends up at a host other than the app's own origin, or the network log shows a 302 Location header pointing to the injected external URL.

name: Firebase / Supabase / BaaS Config Object Exposed in Bundle
description: React apps that use Firebase, Supabase, or AWS Amplify embed the full client config (apiKey, authDomain, projectId, anonKey, identityPoolId) in the JS bundle. Without proper Firestore security rules or RLS policies, these credentials allow unauthenticated reads and writes to the entire database.
runtime_discovery: Fetch all JS chunk URLs. Search response bodies for known BaaS config object shapes: firebaseConfig = { apiKey:, createClient( with url and anonKey, or Amplify.configure({ with aws_user_pools_id. Extract the key values and attempt unauthenticated Firestore/REST API reads against the project.
detection_signal: JS chunk body contains the literal string firebaseConfig or SUPABASE_ANON_KEY alongside a project ID, followed by a successful unauthenticated GET to https://<project>.firebaseio.com/.json or https://<project>.supabase.co/rest/v1/<table> returning non-empty data.

name: IDOR via Client-Controlled Object IDs in API Calls
description: React SPAs pass resource IDs (userId, orderId, documentId) directly from client-side state into API request paths or bodies. Without server-side ownership checks, incrementing or substituting another user's ID returns or modifies their data.
runtime_discovery: Log in as user A, capture API requests containing numeric or UUID resource identifiers (e.g. GET /api/orders/1234). Log in as user B and replay those requests substituting user A's IDs. Compare the responses: if user B receives user A's data, IDOR is confirmed.
detection_signal: Auth-diff: an authenticated request made with user B's session token to a URL containing user A's resource ID returns HTTP 200 with a non-empty body matching user A's data schema, instead of 403 or an empty/error response.

name: Production Source Maps Publicly Accessible
description: When source maps are deployed alongside production bundles without access restrictions, attackers can reconstruct the full original TypeScript/JavaScript source — including business logic, hardcoded values, and internal API endpoint paths not visible in the minified bundle.
runtime_discovery: Fetch each discovered .js chunk URL and check the final comment for //# sourceMappingURL= pointing to a .map file. Request that .map URL directly. If it returns HTTP 200 with a JSON body containing a sources array, source reconstruction is possible.
detection_signal: A .js.map URL returns HTTP 200 Content-Type application/json with a top-level sources array containing recognizable file paths (src/components/, src/api/) and a non-empty sourcesContent array.

name: GraphQL Introspection Enabled in Production
description: React apps backed by GraphQL APIs frequently leave introspection enabled in production, exposing the full schema — all types, queries, mutations, and field names — to unauthenticated or low-privilege callers, which dramatically accelerates targeted attacks.
runtime_discovery: Identify the GraphQL endpoint from network traffic or bundle strings (typically /graphql, /api/graphql). Send a POST with the standard introspection query { __schema { types { name } } }. Parse the response for a full type list.
detection_signal: POST to the GraphQL endpoint with Content-Type application/json body {"query":"{__schema{types{name}}}"} returns HTTP 200 with a data.__schema.types array containing more than 10 entries including built-in scalar types.

name: Sensitive Data Leaked in URL Query Parameters / Hash Fragment
description: React apps that pass tokens, session IDs, or PII through URL query strings or hash fragments expose this data to browser history, Referer headers, server access logs, and third-party analytics scripts loaded on the page.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the app and capture all navigation URLs (including redirects). Inspect query strings and hash fragments for patterns matching tokens (JWT, UUID, long hex), email addresses, phone numbers, or other PII. Also observe OAuth callback URLs for token fragments.
detection_signal: A navigated-to URL contains a query parameter or fragment matching JWT pattern, high-entropy token (32+ hex chars or Base64url), email address regex, or numeric ID combined with PII field name keys (ssn=, dob=, phone=).

name: Insecure postMessage Origin Validation (Wildcard or Missing)
description: React apps that use window.postMessage for cross-origin iframe or tab communication and either post with targetOrigin '*' or receive without checking event.origin allow any page to send or receive sensitive messages, enabling token theft or unauthorized action triggering.
runtime_discovery: Load the app in an iframe (or open as a child window) from a controlled test page. Dispatch postMessage events with crafted payloads (fake auth tokens, action commands) and observe whether the app processes them. Also listen for outgoing postMessage calls with sensitive data and no origin restriction.
detection_signal: A postMessage listener in the app's JS bundle (found via source map or bundle analysis) lacks an event.origin check, OR the scanner's controlled frame receives a postMessage containing token/auth/credential fields when the parent sends a trigger message, OR the app mutates its state in response to an untrusted-origin message.

name: Client-Side Feature Flag / Role Check Bypass
description: React apps that gate premium or admin features by evaluating a feature flag, JWT claim, or role value entirely in the browser allow privilege escalation by modifying localStorage, the Redux store, or the decoded JWT payload (when signature is not re-verified) before the conditional render runs.
runtime_discovery: Identify feature flag keys or role field names from the bundle or network responses. Modify the stored flag value in localStorage, sessionStorage, or the in-memory store via injected script (e.g. localStorage.setItem('flags', JSON.stringify({adminMode: true}))). Reload and observe whether protected UI and the backing API calls become accessible.
detection_signal: After overwriting a flag or role value in client storage, the app renders previously hidden UI elements (confirmed by DOM diff) AND the corresponding API calls succeed with HTTP 200, confirming the server also trusts the client-supplied role.

name: Third-Party Script Loaded Without SRI / From Uncontrolled CDN
description: React apps that inject third-party analytics, chat widget, or tag manager scripts without Subresource Integrity (SRI) hashes allow a compromised CDN or DNS hijack to deliver malicious JavaScript with full DOM and localStorage access, including auth tokens and form data.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the app's HTML responses and collect all <script src=> tags pointing to external origins. For each, check whether the integrity= attribute is present and non-empty. Also inspect dynamically injected scripts by observing the DOM after the page fully loads.
detection_signal: A <script src=https://[third-party-domain]/...> tag is present in the live page DOM without an integrity= attribute containing a sha256- or sha384- hash, AND the domain is not the app's own origin or a first-party CDN path.

name: Angular Client-Side Template Injection (CSTI) via Expression Sandbox Escape
description: User-supplied input is interpolated directly into Angular template expressions (e.g., {{userInput}}) or bound with [innerHTML], allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript via crafted payloads like {{constructor.constructor('alert(1)')()}} in AngularJS or trigger DOM-XSS in Angular 2+ through unsafe bindings.
runtime_discovery: Submit payloads like {{7*7}}, {{constructor.constructor('alert(1)')()}} or into every text input, URL parameter, and search field. Observe the rendered DOM for evaluated arithmetic (49 appearing instead of {{7*7}}) or JS execution.
detection_signal: Response body or live DOM contains '49' when input '{{7*7}}' was submitted; or JavaScript alert fires; or Angular error overlay references 'unsafe value used in a resource URL context' paired with reflected input.

name: Angular Client-Side Route Guard Bypass
description: Angular route guards (canActivate, canLoad) are enforced only in the browser; the API endpoints they protect are often reachable directly. An unauthenticated user who navigates to a guarded route may see a redirect, but the underlying REST calls can still succeed because server-side authorization is missing or insufficient.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the SPA to discover all route paths from the JS bundle (look for RouterModule.forRoot or loadChildren chunks). Issue direct HTTP requests to the backing API endpoints using an unauthenticated or low-privilege session token. Compare HTTP response codes and data payloads between an admin session and the unauthenticated direct call.
detection_signal: Direct API call (e.g., GET /api/admin/users) returns HTTP 200 with full payload while the same call with the unauthenticated browser session returns HTTP 302 to /login — confirming guard bypass. Alternatively, the guarded route renders in the browser with no valid JWT present in localStorage/sessionStorage.

name: Vue v-html Directive XSS
description: The v-html directive renders raw HTML without sanitization. When user-supplied content is bound with v-html, any HTML/JavaScript payload is executed directly in the victim's browser DOM, bypassing Vue's default text-escaping that would otherwise render angle brackets as entities.
runtime_discovery: Identify input fields whose values appear rendered as rich text or formatted content in the UI (e.g., comment bodies, profile bios, message previews). Submit and payloads. Inspect the rendered DOM via DevTools to see if HTML is injected as a node rather than escaped text.
detection_signal: Submitted payload appears in the DOM as an actual element (not <img>) and triggers the onerror handler; or DevTools Elements panel shows raw HTML nodes rather than escaped text content inside a Vue-controlled element.

name: Open Redirect via SPA Router redirect_uri / returnUrl Parameter
description: Angular and Vue routers commonly accept a returnUrl or redirect query parameter after login to restore the user's intended destination. If the SPA navigates to this value without validating it is a same-origin path, an attacker can craft a login URL that redirects victims to an external phishing site after successful authentication.
runtime_discovery: Capture the login redirect URL (e.g., /login?returnUrl=/dashboard). Replace the returnUrl value with https://evil.com or //evil.com and complete authentication. Observe whether the post-login redirect lands on the external domain.
detection_signal: After authentication, the browser Location header or window.location.href contains the attacker-supplied external domain. HTTP 302 response Location value matches the injected URL rather than an allowed same-origin path.

name: Sensitive Tokens Stored in localStorage / sessionStorage
description: SPAs often store JWTs, OAuth access tokens, or API keys in localStorage or sessionStorage, making them accessible to any JavaScript on the page including injected scripts from XSS. A successful XSS then becomes a persistent credential harvest without user interaction.
runtime_discovery: Open browser DevTools Application tab after authenticating. Inspect localStorage and sessionStorage keys for any entries whose values decode as JWTs (header.payload.signature), Bearer tokens, or API key patterns. Also observe Network panel Authorization headers to confirm what was read from storage.
detection_signal: localStorage or sessionStorage contains a key (e.g., access_token, jwt, auth_token) whose value matches a JWT regex (^[A-Za-z0-9-_]+\.[A-Za-z0-9-_]+\.[A-Za-z0-9-_]+$) or an API key pattern. Presence is a finding; no active injection needed.

name: Overly Permissive CORS on SPA API Endpoints
description: API servers backing Angular/Vue SPAs are commonly configured with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * or echo back the request Origin without validation. This lets attacker-controlled pages make credentialed cross-origin requests and read responses, defeating the SPA's same-origin protections.
runtime_discovery: Send preflight and simple cross-origin requests to API endpoints (e.g., GET /api/user/me) with an Origin: https://attacker.com header. Check the response for Access-Control-Allow-Origin mirroring the attacker domain and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true simultaneously.
detection_signal: Response headers contain both Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://attacker.com (reflecting injected origin) and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true on an endpoint that returns sensitive data. Or Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on any endpoint accepting cookies or Authorization headers.

name: Angular Universal SSR — SSRF via Server-Side HTTP Fetches
description: Angular Universal applications perform HTTP requests server-side during pre-rendering. If route parameters or query strings are used unsanitized to construct backend fetch URLs, an attacker can redirect the server's HTTP client to internal network resources (metadata endpoints, internal APIs, cloud IMDSv1).
runtime_discovery: Identify URL parameters that appear to drive content fetching in SSR responses (e.g., ?src=, ?url=, ?apiUrl=). Replace values with http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ or http://localhost:9200/_cat/indices. Observe whether the server-rendered HTML contains cloud metadata or internal service data.
detection_signal: Pre-rendered HTML response body contains cloud metadata strings (ami-id, instance-id, security-credentials/) or internal service signatures (Elasticsearch index listing, Redis info output) after submitting a crafted URL-scheme parameter. Response time increase >1 s vs baseline also indicates a live internal probe.

name: Vuex / Pinia State Exposed via Vue DevTools Protocol
description: Production Vue apps that ship with Vue DevTools hooks enabled (window.__VUE_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__ or __VUE__) expose the full reactive state tree, including auth tokens, user PII, and feature flags, to any browser extension or injected script that speaks the DevTools protocol.
runtime_discovery: In the browser console, evaluate window.__VUE__ or window.__VUE_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__. If present, inspect ._instance.appContext.config.globalProperties.$store.state (Vuex) or enumerate Pinia stores via window.__pinia.state.value. No extension required.
detection_signal: window.__VUE__ or window.__VUE_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__ is defined on the production page (truthy in DevTools console). Subsequent store state read returns auth tokens, session data, or PII objects. Also detectable by scanning the response JS bundle for the string devtools hook installed.

name: JWT 'alg:none' Acceptance on SPA API
description: Some API backends consumed by SPAs accept JWTs with the algorithm field set to 'none', bypassing signature verification entirely. An attacker can craft a token with arbitrary claims (admin role, elevated user ID) and submit it with an empty signature.
runtime_discovery: Capture a valid JWT from localStorage or the Authorization header. Base64-decode and modify the header to {"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}, re-encode the desired payload (e.g., role: admin), append a trailing dot with no signature, and resubmit to protected API endpoints. Observe whether the server accepts the unsigned token.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response (or data change) on a privileged endpoint after submitting a forged alg:none JWT, where the original signed JWT with lower privileges returns HTTP 403. Response body reflects the tampered claim (e.g., admin: true in the JSON).

name: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) on SPA REST/GraphQL Endpoints
description: SPA frameworks expose object identifiers in the URL path and API payloads. Without server-side ownership checks, incrementing or substituting a numeric/UUID resource ID in an API call returns another user's data. SPAs make the attack surface especially visible because route parameters and API calls are observable in the browser network tab.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as User A, capture API calls containing resource IDs (e.g., GET /api/orders/1042). Authenticate as User B in a second session, replay User B's token against User A's resource IDs. Alternatively enumerate IDs sequentially from the observed value.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 with User A's data body when the request uses User B's Bearer token and User A's resource ID. Auth-differential comparison: same endpoint returns different ownership metadata (userId, email) for the two sessions, confirming cross-user access.

name: Content Security Policy Misconfiguration Allowing Inline Script in Angular App
description: Angular and Vue production bundles commonly ship with weak or absent CSP headers, or with unsafe-inline / unsafe-eval in the script-src directive. This negates XSS mitigations that CSP is intended to provide, meaning any XSS payload executes without browser-level blocking.
runtime_discovery: Inspect the Content-Security-Policy response header (or <meta http-equiv=Content-Security-Policy>) on the SPA shell page and API responses. Parse the script-src directive for unsafe-inline, unsafe-eval, wildcard hosts (*), or the absence of CSP entirely.
detection_signal: Response header Content-Security-Policy is absent; or contains script-src directives with 'unsafe-inline', 'unsafe-eval', or * wildcards. Observable purely from response headers of the initial page load — no additional requests needed.

name: DOM-XSS via Vue Router Hash Fragment Reflection
description: Vue applications using hash-mode routing (#/) sometimes display route parameters or hash fragments in the DOM (e.g., as page titles, breadcrumbs, or error messages) without encoding. Because the hash is never sent to the server, server-side WAFs cannot block it, making it a reliable DOM-XSS vector.
runtime_discovery: Append XSS payloads to the URL hash and named route parameters: /#/, /#/page?q=. Observe whether the Vue component renders the raw value into the DOM. Also test history-mode routes if hash mode is not used.
detection_signal: After navigation to a crafted hash URL, browser DevTools Elements panel shows unescaped HTML in a Vue-rendered element (e.g., <h1> contains <script> as a child node rather than escaped text); or JavaScript executes. Console errors referencing 'vue-router' params alongside script execution confirm the vector.

name: GraphQL Introspection Enabled in Production SPA Backend
description: Angular and Vue apps frequently back onto a GraphQL API. When introspection is enabled in production, any user can enumerate the full schema — all types, queries, mutations, and field names — providing a complete attack surface map that accelerates IDOR, privilege escalation, and injection testing.
runtime_discovery: Send a POST to the GraphQL endpoint (commonly /graphql, /api/graphql) with the standard introspection query {__schema{types{name,fields{name}}}}. Check whether the response includes the full schema rather than an error or empty data.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response body contains __schema with a populated types array listing application-specific type names (e.g., User, Order, AdminPanel). Response Content-Type is application/json and the data.__schema.types array length is >10.

name: Auth Token Leaked in Angular Error Logs / Sentry Breadcrumbs
owasp_category: A09:2021 Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
description: Angular HttpInterceptors that log request objects (for debugging) commonly include the full Authorization header in error reports sent to Sentry, Datadog RUM, or a custom logging endpoint. These tokens then appear in third-party dashboards accessible to anyone with logging access.
runtime_discovery: Intercept outbound HTTP requests from the SPA using the browser Network panel. Filter for requests to known error-reporting endpoints (sentry.io, browser.sentry-cdn.com, rum.browser-intake-datadoghq.com). Inspect the POST body for breadcrumb objects containing Authorization, x-api-key, or Cookie header values from previous requests.
detection_signal: Outbound POST to a telemetry/logging endpoint contains a JSON body with a breadcrumbs or data field that includes an Authorization or x-api-key value matching a token observed in the application's own API calls. Observable purely from passive network traffic interception.

name: Client-Side Prototype Pollution in SPA Utility Libraries
owasp_category: A08:2021 Software and Data Integrity Failures
description: Many Angular and Vue apps import utility libraries (lodash.merge, jQuery extend, query-string) that are vulnerable to prototype pollution. An attacker supplies a JSON payload with __proto__ or constructor.prototype keys through URL query strings, form data, or API responses, poisoning Object.prototype and potentially escalating to XSS or logic bypass.
runtime_discovery: Submit payloads containing __proto__[test]=polluted or constructor[prototype][test]=polluted through URL query parameters (e.g., ?__proto__[admin]=true) and form fields processed by vulnerable merge/assign functions. After submission, execute Object.prototype.test in the browser console to check if it equals 'polluted'.
detection_signal: After submitting a __proto__ or constructor.prototype payload via URL parameter or form, browser console evaluation of ({}).test returns 'polluted' rather than undefined. In automated scanning, a subsequent request that reads from a property gated by Object.prototype returns unexpected truthy behavior (e.g., isAdmin becomes true on a non-admin object).

name: Jinja2 Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI)
description: User-controlled input is rendered directly through Jinja2's template engine, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary Python expressions. A payload like {{7*7}} evaluates to 49 in the response body, and escalation payloads can reach os.popen() for RCE.
runtime_discovery: Inject SSTI probe strings ({{7*7}}, ${7*7}, #{7*7}) into every text input, query parameter, URL path segment, HTTP header, and cookie value. Observe response body for evaluated arithmetic. Follow up with class-chain payloads for RCE confirmation.
detection_signal: Response body contains the literal evaluated result of the arithmetic probe (e.g., '49' when '{{7*7}}' was submitted). Distinct from XSS because the evaluation happens server-side before HTML rendering.

name: Flask Werkzeug Debugger Exposed in Production
description: When Flask runs with DEBUG=True, the Werkzeug interactive debugger is accessible at any unhandled exception URL and at /__debugger__. It exposes a Python REPL protected only by a PIN that can be bruteforced or read from server logs, enabling full RCE.
runtime_discovery: Trigger a 500 error by sending malformed input or accessing a nonexistent route. Observe whether the response contains the Werkzeug interactive traceback UI (HTML with 'Werkzeug Debugger' heading or the console icon). Also probe GET /__debugger__ and /console.
detection_signal: HTTP 500 response body contains 'Werkzeug Debugger', 'Traceback (most recent call last)', interactive console iframe, or the response contains 'X-Werkzeug-Debugger' header. The /__debugger__ endpoint returns 200.

name: Flask Session Cookie Signed with Weak or Default SECRET_KEY
description: Flask signs session cookies with HMAC-SHA1 using SECRET_KEY. If SECRET_KEY is a common default ('dev', 'secret', 'flask', empty string) or short, an attacker can forge arbitrary session payloads—escalating privileges, impersonating other users, or bypassing authentication.
runtime_discovery: Capture the session cookie (format: base64.base64.signature). Decode the header+payload (base64 with URL-safe alphabet, no padding). Attempt to re-sign with a wordlist of known weak keys using flask-unsign or manual HMAC. Submit the forged cookie and observe whether the application accepts the modified session data.
detection_signal: A re-signed cookie containing {'role':'admin'} or an elevated user ID is accepted by the server with HTTP 200 and admin-level response content, rather than HTTP 401/403 or a session error page.

name: Flask itsdangerous Algorithm Confusion (alg:none / RS→HS)
description: Older versions of itsdangerous and misconfigured JWT libraries used alongside Flask are susceptible to algorithm confusion: setting the algorithm to 'none' or switching from RS256 to HS256 (using the public key as the HMAC secret) can allow unsigned tokens to be accepted.
runtime_discovery: Decode the session cookie or JWT. Craft a variant with the algorithm header changed to 'none' and the signature stripped. Submit to all authenticated endpoints and observe whether the server accepts the unsigned token.
detection_signal: An unsigned (alg:none) or algorithm-swapped token is accepted by the server (HTTP 200 with user-specific content, no redirect to login), indicating the server does not enforce algorithm validation.

name: Open Redirect via next / redirect_to Parameter
description: Flask login views commonly read a 'next' or 'redirect_to' query parameter to redirect after authentication. Without validation that the target is an internal path, an attacker can craft a login URL that redirects users to a phishing site after successful login.
runtime_discovery: Submit GET /login?next=https://evil.example.com and complete authentication (or observe the redirect even unauthenticated). Check Location header in HTTP 302 response for the external host. Fuzz variations: //evil.example.com, /\evil.example.com, %0d%0ahttps://evil.example.com.
detection_signal: HTTP 302 response with Location header pointing to the attacker-supplied external domain (not just the path). Response does not normalize or reject absolute URLs with external hostnames.

name: Insecure Direct Object Reference on Flask Route Parameters
description: Flask REST routes like /api/users/<int:id>/profile expose numeric or UUID resource identifiers directly. Without ownership checks, any authenticated user can read or modify another user's resource by incrementing or guessing the ID.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as user A, record the ID used in API calls (e.g., /api/orders/1042). Authenticate as user B in a parallel session. From user B's session, request user A's resource URL. Compare response bodies: if user B receives user A's private data with HTTP 200, IDOR is confirmed.
detection_signal: Auth-differential check: user B's session retrieves user A's resource with HTTP 200 and identical private data fields (email, address, order details). Expected behavior: HTTP 403 or sanitized/empty response.

name: Missing CSRF Protection on State-Changing Flask Endpoints
description: Flask views that handle POST/PUT/DELETE without Flask-WTF CSRF tokens or custom header checks are vulnerable to cross-site request forgery. An attacker can trick authenticated users into submitting malicious forms.
runtime_discovery: For each state-changing form or AJAX endpoint, submit the POST request without the CSRF token field (or with a tampered token). Observe whether the server accepts the request (HTTP 200/302) or rejects it (HTTP 400/403). Also check for missing SameSite cookie attribute.
detection_signal: POST request without csrf_token field (or with token set to empty string) returns HTTP 200 or 302 with a success response body, instead of HTTP 400/403 with a CSRF validation error.

name: Reflected XSS via Jinja2 Autoescape Disabled
description: Flask/Jinja2 disables autoescaping by default for non-.html templates and for any template where the developer calls render_template_string() with |safe or autoescape=False. User input echoed in such templates is reflected without HTML encoding, enabling XSS.
runtime_discovery: Inject standard XSS probe strings (, ) into all input vectors (form fields, query params, URL path, headers that get echoed). Check whether the probe is reflected in the response body without encoding.
detection_signal: Response body contains the unencoded probe string (literal < and > characters not converted to </>), and the Content-Type is text/html. Script tag or event handler appears verbatim in the DOM.

name: Werkzeug Debug PIN Derivable from Exposed System Info
description: The Werkzeug debug PIN is deterministically computed from the machine's MAC address, /etc/machine-id, and Python module path. When any of these are exposed (e.g., through LFI, /proc endpoint, error traces, or SSRF), the PIN can be computed offline and used to unlock the interactive console.
runtime_discovery: After confirming debug mode is active, probe for LFI or path traversal to read /proc/net/arp, /proc/self/cgroup, /etc/machine-id, /sys/class/net/eth0/address. Also check whether error tracebacks expose the Flask app's filesystem path. Combine gathered values to compute the PIN formula offline.
detection_signal: HTTP 500 traceback response exposes absolute filesystem path of the Flask app (e.g., '/home/app/app.py'). Additional LFI or SSRF probe returns /etc/machine-id or MAC address content, providing all ingredients for PIN derivation.

name: Path Traversal via Flask send_file / Custom File Serving
description: Flask endpoints that construct file paths from user input and pass them to send_file() or open() without normalization are vulnerable to directory traversal. An attacker can read arbitrary files by supplying ../../etc/passwd as the filename parameter.
runtime_discovery: Locate file-download or static-serving endpoints (e.g., /download?file=report.pdf, /static/user-files/<name>). Submit path traversal payloads: ../../../etc/passwd, ..%2F..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd, and URL-double-encoded variants. Observe whether the response contains file content from outside the intended directory.
detection_signal: Response body contains root: or /bin/bash (from /etc/passwd), or known sensitive file content (application.cfg, .env). HTTP status is 200 and Content-Type matches the served file type.

name: SQL Injection in Flask/SQLAlchemy Raw Query
description: Flask apps using db.engine.execute() or text() with f-strings or % formatting instead of bound parameters are vulnerable to SQL injection. This commonly appears in search endpoints and filter parameters.
runtime_discovery: Inject SQL probe payloads into search/filter query parameters and form fields: single quote ('), OR 1=1--, UNION SELECT NULL--, sleep(5). Observe for HTTP 500 database errors, response content changes, or response timing anomalies (for blind SQLi).
detection_signal: HTTP 500 response containing SQLAlchemy stack trace or database error message (e.g., 'sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError', 'ProgrammingError', 'syntax error near'); OR boolean-based response body differs between ' OR '1'='1 and ' OR '1'='2; OR timing-based probe (sleep/pg_sleep) causes measurable response delay.

name: Flask-CORS Wildcard or Credential-Permissive Misconfiguration
description: Flask apps using Flask-CORS with CORS(app, origins='*', supports_credentials=True) or dynamically reflecting the Origin header allow any website to make credentialed cross-origin requests, enabling CSRF-like attacks against authenticated API endpoints.
runtime_discovery: Send requests to API endpoints with a crafted Origin: https://evil.example.com header. Inspect the response for Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true together. Also check for * on the allow-origin header.
detection_signal: Response headers contain both Access-Control-Allow-Origin matching the supplied attacker origin (not *) AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true simultaneously, or Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on endpoints that set authentication cookies.

name: Session Fixation — Session Not Rotated After Login
