description: Flask apps that do not call login_user() with a fresh session (or manually issue session.clear() before setting user data) retain the pre-login session ID after authentication. An attacker who plants a session cookie on a victim can hijack the session after the victim logs in.
runtime_discovery: Record the session cookie value before login. Submit valid credentials. Record the session cookie value after login. Compare the two values: if they are identical, the session was not rotated and the app is vulnerable to fixation.
detection_signal: The Set-Cookie header value for the session cookie is identical in the pre-authentication response and the post-authentication redirect response. No new session identifier is issued upon successful login.

name: Flask Configuration / Environment Disclosure via Debug Endpoints
description: Flask apps in debug mode or with flask-debugtoolbar/flask-admin carelessly mounted expose routes that leak the full application config (SECRET_KEY, DATABASE_URI, API keys) via JSON or HTML responses at well-known paths.
runtime_discovery: Enumerate common Flask debug/admin paths: /_flask_debug, /admin, /config, /_debug_toolbar, /api/config, /env, /debug. Also check for Flask-Admin at /admin/ and flask-debugtoolbar JavaScript asset inclusion in page source which indicates the toolbar is active.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response body contains 'SECRET_KEY', 'DATABASE_URL', 'SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI', or other uppercase Flask config keys in JSON or HTML. Flask-Admin panel loads without authentication requirement.

name: Flask Session Cookie Missing Secure / HttpOnly / SameSite Flags
description: Flask's default session cookie configuration omits the Secure flag (allowing transmission over HTTP), HttpOnly flag (allowing JavaScript access), and SameSite=Strict/Lax (allowing cross-site submission). Misconfigured cookies enable session theft via network sniffing, XSS, or CSRF.
runtime_discovery: Observe Set-Cookie headers in login and any authenticated response. Parse cookie attributes: check for absence of 'Secure', 'HttpOnly', or 'SameSite' directives. Also check that session cookies are not flagged with SameSite=None without Secure.
detection_signal: Set-Cookie header for the session cookie is missing one or more of: Secure attribute (cookie transmitted over HTTP); HttpOnly attribute (not present in the header string); SameSite=Lax or Strict (absent or set to None without Secure). Observable purely from response header inspection with no active probing needed.

name: Django Admin Interface Publicly Accessible
description: The Django admin interface (/admin/) is left exposed to the internet without additional access controls such as IP allowlisting or a separate auth layer. An attacker who discovers it can brute-force credentials or exploit any admin-side vulnerabilities.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the application for /admin/, /admin/login/, /django-admin/, /staff/. Issue GET requests and observe whether a Django admin login page (HTML containing 'Django administration' or 'django-admin' CSS class) is returned with HTTP 200.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response to GET /admin/login/ with response body containing the string 'Django administration' or a <form> with action='/admin/login/'. Absence of IP-restriction redirect (302 to a deny page) confirms exposure.

name: DRF Browsable API Exposed in Production
description: Django REST Framework's browsable API renderer is left enabled in production. It exposes full schema discovery, endpoint enumeration, and interactive form-submission UI to any authenticated or unauthenticated visitor.
runtime_discovery: Issue GET requests to known API endpoints (e.g. /api/, /api/v1/) with Accept: text/html. If DRF Browsable API is active, the response is a full HTML page with a 'Django REST framework' heading, navigation links, and form inputs for every listed endpoint.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 HTML response containing the string 'Django REST framework' in the <title> or <h1> tag, plus a <nav> element listing API routes. Can be triggered just by visiting any DRF endpoint in a browser (Accept: text/html).

name: DRF Missing Object-Level Permission (IDOR via API)
description: DRF views apply view-level permissions (IsAuthenticated) but omit per-object permission checks (get_object() without check_object_permissions()). User A can read or modify objects belonging to User B by substituting the object ID in the URL.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as User A, record object URLs returned from list endpoints (e.g. /api/orders/42/). Re-issue GET/PATCH/DELETE to those URLs while authenticated as User B. Compare HTTP status codes and body content between the two auth contexts.
detection_signal: Auth-diff probe: User B (unrelated tenant) receives HTTP 200 with identical body to User A's response for /api/<resource>/<id>/ — or HTTP 200/204 on PATCH/DELETE that should have been 403. SequenceMatcher similarity > 0.9 between the two bodies flags the finding.

name: Django DEBUG=True Enabled in Production
description: Running Django with DEBUG=True in production exposes detailed tracebacks — including local variable values, settings keys, and installed file paths — in the HTTP response whenever an unhandled exception occurs.
runtime_discovery: Trigger a 500-class error by submitting malformed input (e.g. a non-integer where an integer is expected on any detail URL), appending invalid query parameters, or requesting a non-existent object ID. Observe the response body for a Django yellow debug page.
detection_signal: HTTP 500 response body containing 'Django Version', 'Traceback (most recent call last)', 'Request Method', 'Request URL', and 'Settings' sections rendered as HTML. The string 'You're seeing this error because you have DEBUG = True' is a definitive signal.

name: Django CSRF Exemption on State-Changing Endpoints
description: Django views or DRF APIViews decorated with @csrf_exempt (or using SessionAuthentication without enforcing CSRF checks) are vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery. An attacker can forge state-changing requests from a third-party origin.
runtime_discovery: Submit a state-changing POST/PUT/DELETE request to a session-authenticated endpoint without a CSRF token (omit X-CSRFToken header and csrfmiddlewaretoken field). If the server returns 200/201/204 instead of 403 Forbidden, CSRF protection is absent.
detection_signal: POST request without X-CSRFToken header and without csrfmiddlewaretoken body param returns HTTP 200/201/204 on a session-cookie-authenticated request. A 403 with body 'CSRF verification failed' would indicate protection is active.

name: DRF JWT Accepts 'none' Algorithm or Weak Secret
description: JWT libraries integrated with DRF (djangorestframework-simplejwt, PyJWT <2.x) may accept tokens signed with the 'none' algorithm or use a weak/default secret, allowing an attacker to forge arbitrary tokens and impersonate any user.
runtime_discovery: Obtain a valid JWT from the login endpoint. Decode the payload (base64), modify the 'sub'/'user_id' to another user's ID, re-sign with algorithm='none' (empty signature), and replay to an authenticated endpoint. Alternatively, attempt HMAC brute-force with common secrets ('secret', 'django-insecure-*').
detection_signal: An API endpoint returns HTTP 200 with a valid resource belonging to a different user_id when presented with a token whose header specifies 'alg':'none' and has an empty signature segment. Or a token signed with a guessable secret validates successfully.

name: DRF Serializer Mass Assignment (Missing read_only / extra_kwargs)
description: DRF serializers that do not declare read_only or use fields='__all__' allow clients to write privileged fields (is_staff, role, balance, owner_id) by including them in the POST/PATCH body. The ORM blindly persists whatever the serializer validates.
runtime_discovery: Capture a POST/PATCH request to a resource creation/update endpoint. Inject extra fields in the JSON body (e.g. 'is_staff': true, 'role': 'admin', 'owner': <another user id>). Observe the response body or fetch the object afterwards to see if the injected value was persisted.
detection_signal: POST/PATCH response body echoes back the injected privileged field with the attacker-supplied value, or a subsequent GET of the resource reflects it. HTTP 201/200 with the submitted value present in the JSON response confirms the finding.

name: Django User Enumeration via Login/Password-Reset Timing or Message
description: Django's built-in authentication views or custom views often return distinct error messages ('No account found with that email' vs 'Incorrect password') or exhibit measurable response-time differences that allow an attacker to enumerate valid usernames or email addresses.
runtime_discovery: Submit login POST requests with a known-valid username and a known-invalid username, comparing response bodies and measuring response times. Repeat for /accounts/password_reset/ with a known email vs a random one and compare redirects or response text.
detection_signal: Distinct response bodies between valid and invalid username/email submissions (e.g. 'Enter a correct username' vs 'Please enter a correct username and password'), or a statistically significant timing difference (>100 ms) between responses for existing vs non-existing accounts on repeated measurements.

name: Django SECRET_KEY Leaked in Response or Headers
description: The Django SECRET_KEY is used to sign sessions, CSRF tokens, and password-reset links. If it is accidentally echoed in a debug traceback, a verbose error JSON, a config-dump endpoint, or an /api/settings/ endpoint, an attacker can forge any session cookie or CSRF token.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the application for config-dump or settings-exposure endpoints (/api/settings/, /debug/, /env/). Trigger 500 errors via malformed input and inspect tracebacks. Search all observed responses for patterns matching Django's insecure key format or high-entropy 50-character strings.
detection_signal: Response body containing a string matching the regex `django-insecure-[a-z0-9!@#$%^&*()]{40,}` or a JSON key named 'SECRET_KEY' with a high-entropy value. Also fires when the Django debug page HTML includes 'SECRET_KEY' in the Settings table.

name: DRF List Endpoint Returns All Objects Across Tenants (Missing Queryset Scoping)
description: DRF list views that call Model.objects.all() without filtering by the authenticated user's tenant/ownership return every object in the database to any authenticated user. This is a data-leak variant of BOLA often missed because no ID manipulation is needed.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as User A and call a list endpoint (GET /api/users/, /api/orders/). Note object IDs returned. Authenticate as User B (different tenant) and call the same endpoint. Compare the two response bodies for cross-tenant objects appearing in both lists.
detection_signal: Auth-diff: response bodies for User A and User B on the same list endpoint share one or more object IDs or contain objects whose owner/tenant field belongs to the other user. Body similarity > 0.5 with cross-tenant objects present is a strong signal.

name: Django CORS Wildcard with credentials=true (django-cors-headers Misconfiguration)
description: django-cors-headers misconfigured with CORS_ALLOW_ALL_ORIGINS=True alongside CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS=True violates the CORS spec expectation, but some browsers/proxies still honour it, and it indicates that the server will issue Access-Control-Allow-Origin: <reflected origin> with credentials allowed, enabling cross-origin cookie theft.
runtime_discovery: Issue a preflight OPTIONS request to an API endpoint with an arbitrary Origin header (e.g. Origin: https://evil.example.com). Inspect the response headers for Access-Control-Allow-Origin and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials values.
detection_signal: OPTIONS response includes both Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com (origin reflected or wildcard *) AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. Wildcard with credentials is the highest-severity variant; reflected arbitrary origin with credentials also flags.

name: Django Admin Custom Action Without Superuser Check
description: Django admin custom actions registered via ModelAdmin.actions do not inherit superuser-only constraints by default. A staff user with limited permissions can invoke bulk-action endpoints directly if the action handler does not call has_permission().
runtime_discovery: Log in to /admin/ as a non-superuser staff account. Discover custom action names from the admin changelist HTML (they appear in the <select name='action'> dropdown). POST to the changelist URL with action=<action_name>&_selected_action[]=<id> to invoke the action directly.
detection_signal: POST to /admin/<app>/<model>/ with action=<custom_action> and a selected object ID returns HTTP 302 (success redirect) rather than 403 when submitted under a staff-but-not-superuser session. The resulting database state change (observable via GET afterwards) confirms execution.

name: Django Password-Reset Poisoning via Host Header Injection
description: Django's password-reset flow uses the Host header to construct the reset link URL. If ALLOWED_HOSTS is misconfigured or the load balancer passes an attacker-controlled Host header, the emailed link points to an attacker domain, allowing token theft.
runtime_discovery: Submit a POST to /accounts/password_reset/ (or equivalent) with a spoofed Host header (Host: evil.example.com) and an email address you control. Examine the received password-reset email to see if the link domain matches the injected Host value.
detection_signal: The application accepts the POST and returns HTTP 200/302 without rejecting the spoofed Host value (no 400 Bad Request or SuspiciousOperation response). Confirmation requires observing the emailed link, but the absence of host validation in the HTTP response is the runtime-observable signal.

name: DRF 400 Validation Errors Leaking ORM Field Names and Choices
description: DRF serializer validation errors returned verbatim to clients expose internal model field names, ForeignKey targets, and choice labels that were never intended to be part of the public API contract, aiding attacker enumeration and mass-assignment attacks.
runtime_discovery: Submit a POST/PATCH request with intentionally invalid or missing required fields and observe the 400 response JSON. Look for field names, choice lists, or relational identifiers (e.g. 'user_id', 'tenant_uuid') in the error detail keys that are not documented in the public API spec.
detection_signal: HTTP 400 JSON response body with a top-level key structure mirroring the serializer's field names. Error messages like 'Invalid pk \"X\" - object does not exist' reveal FK field names and table existence. Presence of undocumented field names in error keys compared to the published OpenAPI schema is the flag.

name: Django Session ID Not Rotated After Login
description: If Django's login view does not call request.session.cycle_key() (or uses a custom auth flow that skips session rotation), a pre-authentication session ID remains valid post-login, enabling session fixation attacks.
runtime_discovery: Capture the session cookie value from a GET to the login page (unauthenticated). Submit valid credentials via POST /login/. Compare the Set-Cookie session value in the POST response to the pre-login session value. If they are identical, session fixation is present.
detection_signal: The sessionid cookie value in the POST /login/ response Set-Cookie header matches the sessionid cookie that was issued on the unauthenticated GET to /login/. Session rotation would produce a different sessionid value after successful authentication.

name: FastAPI Auto-Generated Docs Exposed in Production
description: FastAPI enables /docs (Swagger UI) and /redoc (ReDoc) and the raw OpenAPI schema at /openapi.json by default. In production these endpoints reveal every route, parameter name, request/response schema, and any security scheme definition, giving an attacker a complete attack surface map without any prior crawling.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the target; probe /docs, /redoc, /openapi.json, /api/docs, /api/openapi.json. Fetch /openapi.json and parse the JSON for paths, security schemes, and server variables. No auth required to read the schema on a misconfigured app.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 on /openapi.json returns a JSON body with top-level keys 'openapi', 'info', and 'paths'. /docs returns an HTML page whose title contains 'Swagger UI'. /redoc returns a page containing 'ReDoc'. All are unauthenticated.

name: FastAPI Route Missing Depends(get_current_user) Auth Guard
description: FastAPI authentication is opt-in per route via Depends(). A developer who forgets the dependency on a sensitive endpoint leaves it fully open. This is easy to miss during refactoring and does not produce any startup error.
runtime_discovery: Crawl authenticated session to discover routes from /openapi.json. Then replay each request without the Authorization / Cookie header and compare HTTP status codes. Any route that returns 200 without credentials is a candidate finding.
detection_signal: Authenticated request returns 200; unauthenticated replay of the identical request also returns 200 with the same or richer response body. Differential: auth-diff response bodies match or the unauthenticated body contains more data (not a 401/403).

name: IDOR via FastAPI Path Parameter (Object-Level Authorization Missing)
description: Routes like GET /users/{user_id}/profile or GET /orders/{order_id} authenticate the caller but do not verify that the resource belongs to the caller. An attacker increments or guesses the path parameter to access other users' data.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as user A, record a resource ID from a normal response. Authenticate as user B (auth-diff), attempt to access user A's resource ID using user B's session. Compare response: if user B receives user A's data, IDOR is confirmed.
detection_signal: User B's request to /users/<user_A_id>/profile returns HTTP 200 with user A's PII fields (name, email, etc.) rather than 403/404. Response body under user B's session contains fields identical to the response seen under user A's session.

name: Pydantic Model Mass Assignment / Over-Posting
description: If an endpoint uses a Pydantic model as the request body and that model includes privileged fields (is_admin, role, balance), a client can POST those fields and they will be deserialized and persisted if the developer passes model.dict() directly to the ORM without an allowlist.
runtime_discovery: Submit a POST/PUT request body that includes extra fields beyond the documented schema — specifically guessing privileged field names like 'is_admin', 'role', 'credits', 'verified'. Observe whether the response echoes the injected field or whether a subsequent GET reflects the modified value.
detection_signal: POST body includes 'is_admin': true. Subsequent GET /users/me returns 'is_admin': true in the response JSON, or a privilege-gated endpoint that previously returned 403 now returns 200 with the modified session.

name: Overly Permissive CORS (allow_origins=['*'] with allow_credentials)
description: FastAPI's CORSMiddleware with allow_origins=['*'] and allow_credentials=True is an invalid/broken configuration (browsers block it), but allow_origins=['*'] without credentials still lets any origin read API responses, enabling cross-origin data theft from authenticated sessions in some contexts. Additionally, apps that echo the Origin header back in Access-Control-Allow-Origin are vulnerable to full CORS bypass.
runtime_discovery: Send a preflight OPTIONS request with an arbitrary Origin header (Origin: https://evil.example.com). Inspect the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header. Also send a cross-origin request with Origin set and observe whether the response reflects it.
detection_signal: OPTIONS response contains 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com' (reflected origin) alongside 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true'. Or GET response returns 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *' on an endpoint that returns user-specific data.

name: JWT Algorithm Confusion / None Algorithm Acceptance
description: FastAPI apps using python-jose or PyJWT may accept JWTs with 'alg: none' or allow an RS256-signed token to be verified as HS256 using the public key as the HMAC secret if algorithms= is not explicitly restricted. This allows forging arbitrary JWTs without knowing the private key.
runtime_discovery: Obtain a valid JWT from a login response. Craft a modified token with 'alg': 'none' and no signature (trailing dot) with elevated claims (is_admin: true, role: 'admin'). Submit to protected endpoints. Also try re-signing with HS256 using the app's public key obtained from /openapi.json or /.well-known/jwks.json.
detection_signal: A request bearing a forged 'alg: none' JWT receives HTTP 200 instead of 401/403. Or: a JWT re-signed with HS256 using the public key bytes is accepted. Response body matches a privileged user's data structure.

name: Python Traceback / Debug Info Leaked in 500 Response
description: When a FastAPI app is run with debug=True or without a global exception handler, unhandled exceptions return a full Python stack trace in the HTTP response body, leaking file paths, library versions, environment variable names, and sometimes credentials passed to failing constructors.
runtime_discovery: Submit malformed inputs to every endpoint — type mismatches, boundary values, null bytes, oversized payloads, missing required fields. Look for 500 responses. Inspect the response body for Python exception text, file path patterns, and class/module names.
detection_signal: HTTP 500 response body contains 'Traceback (most recent call last):', absolute filesystem paths matching '/app/' or '/home/', or Python exception class names like 'sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError'. Content-Type is text/html or text/plain rather than application/json.

name: SQL Injection via Raw Query Fallback in FastAPI/SQLAlchemy Routes
description: FastAPI routes that accept query parameters and pass them to text() or execute() raw SQL calls are vulnerable to SQL injection. The ORM is often used for CRUD but developers fall back to raw SQL for search, filter, and reporting endpoints.
runtime_discovery: Fuzz query string and body parameters with SQL metacharacters: single quote, UNION SELECT, comment sequences (-- , /**/), and boolean payloads (1=1, 1=2). Look for response content differences, error messages, or timing differences between true and false payloads.
detection_signal: Boolean-based: payload '1 AND 1=1' returns full result set; '1 AND 1=2' returns empty set or different row count. Error-based: response body contains 'sqlite3.OperationalError', 'psycopg2.errors', 'ProgrammingError', or partial SQL statement. Time-based: SLEEP(5)/pg_sleep(5) causes >= 5 second response delay.

name: Pydantic Type Coercion Bypasses Semantic Validation
description: Pydantic v1 silently coerces strings to integers, booleans, and other types. A field annotated as int will accept '0; DROP TABLE users--' — Pydantic raises a validation error, but a field annotated as str with no further validator accepts any string. Business-logic validators using @validator that check a str field may be bypassed by supplying a numeric type that coerces to the validated string form.
runtime_discovery: Submit requests with type-confused payloads: send a number where a string is expected, send a string for a boolean field ('true', '1', 'yes'), send an array where a scalar is expected. Observe whether the app accepts the coerced value and reflects it in business logic (e.g. a boolean field sent as 'admin' string).
detection_signal: POST with 'active': 'yes' returns 200 and subsequent GET shows 'active': true. POST with 'amount': '100.00; echo pwned' triggers a 500 with database/processing error rather than a 422 validation error. Response diverges from the OpenAPI schema's documented type.

name: SSRF via User-Controlled URL Parameter in FastAPI Endpoint
description: FastAPI endpoints that accept a URL parameter and use httpx.AsyncClient or requests.get to fetch it are vulnerable to SSRF. Attackers can probe internal services, cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254), or force connections to internal-only hosts.
runtime_discovery: Identify parameters named url, callback, webhook, redirect, endpoint, src, or target. Submit payloads targeting cloud metadata: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/, http://[::ffff:169.254.169.254]/, and Burp Collaborator/interactsh callback URLs. Observe response time, error messages, and whether response body contains cloud metadata content.
detection_signal: Request with url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ returns HTTP 200 with body containing 'ami-id' or 'instance-type' strings. Or: response contains an internal hostname resolved successfully (DNS lookup observed on out-of-band callback). Or: a 500 with 'Connection refused' to an internal IP vs timeout to an external IP — timing/error differential reveals internal network topology.

name: Missing Security Headers on FastAPI Responses
description: FastAPI does not add security headers by default. Missing headers like X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, and Referrer-Policy leave the app open to clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and downgrade attacks.
runtime_discovery: Fetch any endpoint and inspect the response headers. Check for presence/absence of security headers. No active probing beyond a single GET request to the root and a few data endpoints.
detection_signal: Response headers lack one or more of: 'X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff', 'X-Frame-Options: DENY' (or SAMEORIGIN), 'Content-Security-Policy', 'Strict-Transport-Security', 'Referrer-Policy'. Absence is the signal — purely header inspection on a normal response.

name: No Rate Limiting on Authentication / Sensitive Endpoints
description: FastAPI has no built-in rate limiting. Without slowapi, a Redis-backed limiter, or an upstream gateway, login, password-reset, OTP verification, and registration endpoints accept unlimited requests, enabling brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks.
runtime_discovery: Send 50-100 rapid successive POST requests to /login or /auth/token with varying passwords. Observe whether the app returns 429 Too Many Requests at any point, introduces increasing latency, or locks the account. Absence of 429 across the burst is the finding.
detection_signal: 100 consecutive POST /login requests all return either 200 or 401 — never 429. Response latency remains flat across the burst (no exponential backoff). No 'Retry-After' or 'X-RateLimit-*' headers appear in any response.

name: API Token / JWT Passed in Query String (Server Log Exposure)
description: FastAPI's OAuth2PasswordBearer and APIKeyQuery schemes accept tokens via query parameter (?token=... or ?api_key=...). Tokens in query strings are logged in server access logs, browser history, Referer headers, and proxies — leading to credential theft from log access.
runtime_discovery: Crawl all endpoints and observe the URL patterns used for authentication. Check whether any authenticated endpoint is reachable with the token in the query string rather than (or in addition to) the Authorization header.
detection_signal: Requests to /api/data?token=<jwt> or /export?api_key=<key> return HTTP 200 authenticated responses. The OpenAPI schema for the endpoint shows an APIKeyQuery security scheme. The full token value is visible in the request URL captured by the proxy.

name: FastAPI BackgroundTask / Celery Task ID Enumeration
description: FastAPI endpoints that offload work to BackgroundTasks or Celery and return a task_id in the response may expose a /tasks/{task_id}/status endpoint with no ownership check. Any authenticated user can poll another user's task to observe its status, result payload, or input parameters.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as user A, trigger a long-running action (export, import, report generation) and capture the returned task_id. Authenticate as user B (auth-diff) and request /tasks/<user_A_task_id>/status or /jobs/<id>. Compare: if user B receives user A's task status or output data, IDOR is confirmed.
detection_signal: User B's GET /tasks/<user_A_task_id> returns HTTP 200 with a result payload matching the input parameters submitted by user A (e.g. user A's email address, file name, or query parameters visible in the result). No 403/404 returned.

name: Environment / Config Variables Echoed in FastAPI Health or Debug Endpoints
description: FastAPI apps using pydantic-settings often expose a /health, /status, /debug, or /config endpoint that serializes the settings object for observability purposes. If the settings model includes secret fields (DATABASE_URL, SECRET_KEY, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY) and they are not explicitly excluded, the full secrets are returned in the response.
runtime_discovery: Probe common diagnostic endpoints: /health, /healthz, /status, /debug, /info, /config, /env, /settings, /metrics, /_debug. Inspect response bodies for key-value patterns matching secret field names or high-entropy strings.
detection_signal: GET /health returns a JSON body containing fields named 'database_url', 'secret_key', 'api_key', or 'password' with non-placeholder string values. Or response contains a high-entropy string (Shannon entropy > 4.0 bits/char) adjacent to a key name matching known credential patterns (key, token, secret, password, dsn).

name: Mass Assignment — Privilege Escalation via Admin/Role Field
description: Rails models without strict strong-params permit lists allow attackers to POST extra fields (e.g., `user[admin]=1`, `user[role]=admin`) that map directly to protected database columns. Even with strong_parameters enabled, misconfigured `permit!` calls or overly broad `permit(:role)` admit escalation.
runtime_discovery: Submit a registration or profile-update form and inject extra parameters: `user[admin]=true`, `user[role]=admin`, `user[is_staff]=1`. Compare the authenticated session's capabilities before and after; re-fetch the user profile endpoint as the same account to see if the field was persisted. Auth-diff by checking whether the modified account can reach admin-only routes that a normal account cannot.
detection_signal: POST to `/users` or `/account` with appended params `user[admin]=1` returns HTTP 200/302 without a parameter-not-permitted error; subsequent GET to `/users/:id` or `/api/v1/me` returns `"admin":true` or `"role":"admin"` in the JSON response body, confirming the write was accepted.

name: Default Catch-All Route Exposing Unintended Controller Actions
description: Legacy Rails apps may include the catch-all route `match ':controller(/:action(/:id))'` in routes.rb, exposing every public controller method as a URL without explicit authentication or authorization guards. This surfaces developer or admin actions never intended to be public.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the app and enumerate URL patterns by guessing controller/action pairs: `/admin/index`, `/users/destroy/1`, `/orders/export`. Look for any action name that differs from the REST standard seven (index/show/new/create/edit/update/destroy). Attempt HTTP GET/POST to guessed routes and observe whether a 200 or redirect-with-content is returned instead of a 404.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response body with HTML or JSON content for a guessed path like `/<controller>/<non-restful-action>` that is not linked anywhere in the crawled surface; the response differs from a genuine 404 page (different status code, different Content-Length, or recognizable application chrome).

name: IDOR via Unscoped ActiveRecord Lookups (Direct Object Reference)
description: Controllers that call `Model.find(params[:id])` without scoping to `current_user` allow any authenticated user to access any record by incrementing or fuzzing the ID. Rails' numeric auto-increment IDs make these trivially enumerable.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as User A, capture a resource URL such as `/invoices/42`. Authenticate as User B (different account), send a GET to `/invoices/41`, `/invoices/43`, `/invoices/1`. Auth-diff: if User B receives User A's data with a 200 instead of a 403/404, an IDOR is confirmed.
detection_signal: User B's authenticated GET to `/resource/:id` owned by User A returns HTTP 200 with the resource body (name, email, data fields) that matches what User A sees; User B's own resource returns the same HTTP 200 structure, confirming no ownership enforcement.

name: Strong Params `permit!` Whitelisting Everything
description: Calling `params.permit!` or `params.require(:user).permit!` whitelists all submitted parameters without restriction, functionally reverting Rails 4+ strong_parameters protection to Rails 3 behavior. Any model attribute — including computed or protected columns — becomes writable.
runtime_discovery: Submit a form or JSON API body with extra fields commonly present in Rails user models: `[admin, role, confirmed, locked, stripe_customer_id, api_key]`. If a subset of those fields appear in the subsequent GET response or cause a behavior change (unlocked account, confirmed email without verification flow), `permit!` or an overly broad permit is in use.
detection_signal: POST body containing `user[confirmed]=true` or `user[locked]=false` returns HTTP 200/302 and a follow-up GET to the user profile shows `"confirmed":true` or a changed field value; alternatively, the app does not return a Rails `ActionController::UnpermittedParameters` log error translated to a 400 for novel fields.

name: Missing or Bypassable CSRF Protection on State-Changing Endpoints
description: Rails includes CSRF protection via `protect_from_forgery`, but controllers that skip this callback or accept JSON content-type requests without verifying the token expose state-changing actions to cross-site request forgery. API-mode apps that incorrectly share cookie sessions with browser clients are a common pattern.
runtime_discovery: Crawl all POST/PATCH/DELETE forms and API endpoints. For each, replay the request without the `authenticity_token` field or `X-CSRF-Token` header. If the server still processes the state change (returns 200 or 302 instead of 422 Unprocessable Entity), CSRF protection is absent or skipped.
detection_signal: POST to a state-changing endpoint (password change, email update, account delete) without any `authenticity_token` parameter or `X-CSRF-Token` header returns HTTP 200 or 302 (success redirect) rather than HTTP 422 with a `ActionController::InvalidAuthenticityToken` error.

name: Open Redirect via Unsafe `redirect_to params[:return_to]`
description: Rails controllers frequently accept a `return_to`, `redirect`, or `next` URL parameter and pass it directly to `redirect_to`, which will follow an absolute URL to an external host if the value is not validated against an allowlist. This enables phishing and OAuth token-stealing flows.
runtime_discovery: Identify login, logout, and OAuth callback endpoints. Append `?return_to=https://evil.example.com` (or `?redirect=//evil.example.com`). After completing the authentication flow, observe whether the browser or the HTTP response Location header points to the external domain.
detection_signal: HTTP 302 response Location header contains an external host (e.g., `https://evil.example.com`) after submitting `?return_to=https://evil.example.com` to a login or post-auth endpoint; the redirect target differs from the application's own hostname.

name: SQL Injection via ActiveRecord String Interpolation
description: ActiveRecord's `where`, `order`, `group`, and `select` methods accept raw SQL strings. Developers who interpolate user input directly (`where("name = '#{params[:name]}'")` or `order(params[:sort])`) bypass the parameterized query protection that the `?` placeholder syntax provides.
runtime_discovery: In search, filter, and sort query parameters, inject SQL payloads: a single quote `'`, `' OR '1'='1`, `1; --`, and `1 ORDER BY 1--`. For sort parameters specifically try `name ASC, (SELECT SLEEP(2))--`. Observe whether the response produces a Rails SQL error (500 with ActiveRecord::StatementInvalid), returns unexpected extra rows, or shows measurable time delays.
detection_signal: Injecting `'` into a query parameter produces HTTP 500 with body containing `ActiveRecord::StatementInvalid`, `PG::SyntaxError`, or `SQLite3::Exception`; or time-based payload `'; SELECT pg_sleep(3); --` causes response time to increase by 3+ seconds compared to baseline.

name: Reflected/Stored XSS via `raw`, `html_safe`, or Missing ERB Escaping
description: Rails' ERB templates auto-escape output by default, but developers who call `.html_safe`, `raw()`, or `content_tag` with unescaped user content reintroduce XSS. This is detectable at runtime wherever user-supplied strings are reflected back in responses.
runtime_discovery: Submit the string `<script>alert(1)</script>` (and URL-encoded/double-encoded variants) into every text input, URL parameter, and HTTP header that appears to be reflected in the response. Check name fields, search boxes, profile fields, and error messages. Also attempt stored XSS by saving the payload and retrieving it in a subsequent GET.
detection_signal: The literal string `<script>alert(1)</script>` (unencoded, not `<script>`) appears in the HTTP response body with Content-Type `text/html`, indicating the value was rendered without HTML-escaping.

name: User Enumeration via Devise Login/Reset Timing or Message Differences
description: Default Devise configurations return different error messages (`Invalid email or password` vs. `Email not found`) or exhibit measurable response-time differences for existing vs. non-existing accounts during login and password-reset flows, enabling attacker enumeration of valid usernames.
runtime_discovery: Submit the login form with a known-valid email and wrong password; record status code, response body text, and response time. Repeat with a non-existent email. Compare the two responses for any difference in message text (e.g., `Invalid email or password` vs `No account found`), HTTP status, or response latency (>100ms delta indicates BCrypt only runs for existing accounts).
detection_signal: Two login attempts return different response body text or differ in response time by more than 150ms: known email + bad password produces one string (`Invalid email or password`) while unknown email produces another (`Email not found`), or the timing delta indicates BCrypt computation only occurs on the valid-account path.

name: JSON API Mass Assignment via Unpermitted Nested Attributes
description: Rails controllers that accept `accepts_nested_attributes_for` or JSON:API payloads may expose nested model writes when the strong-params permit list includes `relationships` or nested hashes broadly. An attacker can write to associated models (e.g., change another user's data via a nested `user_attributes` key) without direct access to that model's endpoint.
runtime_discovery: On endpoints that accept nested JSON (e.g., `POST /projects` with `included` or `relationships`), inject additional nested attribute blocks targeting associated models: `{"data":{"relationships":{"owner":{"data":{"id":"2"}}}}}`. Check whether the ownership of the created/updated resource changed to the injected ID by fetching the object afterward.
detection_signal: After submitting a nested JSON payload with a foreign key or relationship ID pointing to another user's resource, a GET to the created/updated object returns the injected association ID (e.g., `owner_id: 2` rather than the authenticated user's ID), confirming the nested attribute write was accepted.

name: Insecure Cookie-Based Session — Known/Weak Secret Key Base
description: Rails' cookie-based session is signed (and optionally encrypted) with `secret_key_base`. Applications using a default, publicly committed, or short secret allow an attacker to forge arbitrary session cookies, escalating to any user including admins.
runtime_discovery: Capture the session cookie (`_session_store` or app-named cookie). Check if it decodes as a base64 JSON blob without encryption (older config) or a MessageEncryptor ciphertext. Attempt to decode the cookie using known default keys (e.g., `secret`). Separately, fuzz the session with a cookie containing serialized `{"warden.user.user.key":[[1],"$2a$..."]}`-style payloads to test whether an arbitrary user ID is accepted.
detection_signal: Sending a crafted session cookie with a forged `user_id` returns HTTP 200 with response body containing the target user's email, name, or account data — confirming the signature was accepted and the server trusts the cookie value without re-validating identity.

name: Path Traversal via `send_file` / `send_data` with User-Controlled Path
description: Controllers that call `send_file(Rails.root.join('uploads', params[:filename]))` without sanitizing the filename parameter allow `../` traversal to read arbitrary files accessible to the Rails process, including `config/database.yml`, `config/credentials.yml.enc`, and `.env` files.
runtime_discovery: Identify file download endpoints that accept a filename or path parameter (e.g., `/files/download?name=report.pdf`, `/attachments/123?filename=...`). Inject traversal sequences: `../../config/database.yml`, `%2e%2e%2fconfig%2fdatabase.yml`, `....//config//database.yml`. Check response Content-Type and body for YAML/env file content.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response with Content-Type `application/octet-stream` or `text/plain` whose body contains YAML keys like `adapter:`, `database:`, `password:` or environment variable assignments like `DATABASE_URL=`, indicating the server read a config file rather than the intended upload.

name: Rails Verbose Error Pages Enabled in Production (config.consider_all_requests_local)
description: When `config.consider_all_requests_local = true` is left in `production.rb`, Rails renders full stack traces, source code snippets, environment variables, and request parameters in the browser on any unhandled exception, exposing internal architecture and secrets.
runtime_discovery: Trigger an application error by submitting malformed input to a known endpoint (e.g., passing a string where an integer is expected for an ID, submitting an invalid enum value). Also probe for 500s with boundary inputs like extremely large payloads, null bytes, and invalid UTF-8 sequences. Observe whether the error page contains a stack trace with file paths and source code.
detection_signal: HTTP 500 response body contains Rails error page markers: `<h1>` with the exception class name (e.g., `ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound`), a source code window showing `.rb` file lines, the full file system path under `/app/` or `/var/`, or the `Request Parameters` dump listing environment variables.

name: Exposed `/rails/info/routes` and `/rails/info/properties` in Production
description: Rails development helpers including `/rails/info/routes`, `/rails/info/properties`, and `/rails/mailers` expose the full application route table, framework version, middleware stack, and environment info. These are restricted to localhost by default but are sometimes left open in containerized or misconfigured production deployments.
runtime_discovery: Send unauthenticated GET requests to `/rails/info/routes`, `/rails/info/properties`, `/rails/info`, `/rails/mailers`. Also try `/rails/info/routes.json`. Check response for route table HTML (table with `Helper`, `HTTP Verb`, `Path`, `Controller#Action` columns) or a properties page listing `Ruby version`, `RubyGems version`, `Rack version`, `Rails version`.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response to `GET /rails/info/routes` with HTML body containing a table of controller action mappings (e.g., `users#index`, `admin#dashboard`), or `GET /rails/info/properties` returning a properties table showing `Rails version:` and `Ruby version:` values — confirming the info controller is accessible without authorization.

name: Insecure Locale/Tenant Parameter Switching (Subdomain or `locale` Param Hijack)
description: Multi-tenant or multi-locale Rails apps that derive the current tenant or locale from a URL parameter or subdomain without validating it against the authenticated user's permitted tenants allow privilege escalation by simply changing the `locale`, `account_id`, or subdomain to another tenant's identifier.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as a user in tenant A (e.g., subdomain `acme.app.com` or `?account_id=1`). Then request the same resource endpoint substituting another tenant's identifier: `rival.app.com/dashboard` or `?account_id=2`. Auth-diff: if the response returns tenant B's data rather than a 403/404, the tenant boundary is not enforced at the data layer.
detection_signal: Changing the `account_id` query parameter or subdomain in an authenticated request returns HTTP 200 with a response body containing a different tenant's organization name, user list, or data records — distinct from what the authenticated user's own tenant returns — confirming cross-tenant data access.

name: Laravel APP_DEBUG=true Information Disclosure
description: When APP_DEBUG is set to true in a Laravel production app, unhandled exceptions expose a full stack trace including file paths, environment variable values, SQL queries, and sometimes secret keys directly in the HTTP response body.
runtime_discovery: Trigger an error by sending a malformed request (invalid route parameter, bad JSON body, or a non-existent resource). Observe whether the response body contains a Whoops/Ignition stack trace page instead of a generic error page.
detection_signal: HTTP 500 response body contains 'Whoops\Something went wrong', 'Ignition', 'APP_KEY', full absolute filesystem paths like '/var/www/html/...', or SQL query strings with table names exposed in the HTML.

name: Eloquent Mass Assignment Privilege Escalation
description: Laravel Eloquent models that use $fillable wildcards or omit $guarded allow attackers to inject arbitrary model attributes (e.g., is_admin, role, email_verified_at) via form POST or JSON body fields that the application never intended to accept.
runtime_discovery: Intercept a user registration or profile-update form POST. Inject extra fields such as is_admin=1, role=admin, or email_verified_at=2024-01-01 into the request body. Re-fetch the authenticated user profile and compare field values before and after.
detection_signal: After injecting extra fields, the GET /api/user (or equivalent) response reflects the injected attribute (e.g., '"is_admin":true') or the app grants elevated permissions not available to a baseline account.

name: Laravel Debugbar / Telescope Endpoint Exposed in Production
description: The barryvdh/laravel-debugbar package injects a debug toolbar into HTML responses and exposes /_debugbar/open?max=20 for raw JSON logs. Laravel Telescope exposes /telescope/requests and /telescope/queries. Both endpoints can reveal SQL queries with bound parameters, session tokens, env vars, and internal request metadata.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the app and probe /_debugbar/open, /_debugbar/assets/stylesheets, /telescope, /telescope/api/requests, and /telescope/api/queries without authentication. Also inspect HTML source for the debugbar injection div.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 on /_debugbar/open?max=20 returns JSON with 'queries', 'session', or 'env' keys. /telescope/api/requests returns a JSON array of recent HTTP requests including headers and payloads.

name: phpinfo() Page Exposed
description: A publicly accessible phpinfo() output page reveals PHP version, server software, loaded extensions, environment variables (including those set in .env files loaded via putenv), and all php.ini directives — frequently exposing database credentials, API keys, and internal network topology.
runtime_discovery: Probe common phpinfo paths: /phpinfo.php, /info.php, /php_info.php, /test.php, /?phpinfo=1. Also check server-info paths like /server-info and /_info.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response body contains the string 'PHP Version' followed by a version number and an HTML table with 'PHP Credits' or 'Environment' sections. The Environment table may contain DATABASE_URL, APP_KEY, or similar values.

name: Laravel .env File Accessible via Web Root
description: When the web server document root is set to the application root rather than the /public subdirectory, the .env file is served as a static text file, exposing APP_KEY, DB_PASSWORD, and all API tokens in plaintext.
runtime_discovery: Probe /.env, /.env.backup, /.env.local, /.env.production, /.env.bak, and /.env.old directly with a GET request.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response with Content-Type text/plain or no content-type, and body contains lines matching APP_KEY=base64:, DB_PASSWORD=, MAIL_PASSWORD=, or AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=.

name: Laravel Storage Public Symlink Exposes Private Files
description: Laravel's php artisan storage:link creates a /public/storage symlink to storage/app/public. If sensitive files (user uploads, exports, invoices) are mistakenly stored under storage/app/public instead of the private disk, they become directly downloadable without authentication by guessing or brute-forcing filenames.
runtime_discovery: After uploading or receiving a file as an authenticated user, note the file URL. Log out and attempt to access the URL unauthenticated. Also enumerate /storage/ for directory listing or probe predictable filenames (UUIDs, incremental IDs, YYYY/MM/DD/ paths).
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response for /storage/<filename> when no auth cookie is present. Alternatively, HTTP 200 on /storage/ with directory index HTML listing filenames.

name: PHP SQL Injection via Error-Based Leakage
description: PHP applications that interpolate user input directly into SQL queries without prepared statements are vulnerable to SQL injection. Error-based injection is detectable at runtime when database errors are printed or returned in responses, leaking table names, column names, and data.
runtime_discovery: For each input field and URL parameter, inject a single quote ('). Observe whether the response changes in status code, body length, or content. Try payloads like 1' OR '1'='1 and 1 AND 1=2. For error-based leakage, try ') OR extractvalue(1,concat(0x7e,database()))-- on MySQL targets.
detection_signal: Response body contains 'You have an error in your SQL syntax', 'mysql_fetch', 'ORA-', 'pg_query()', or 'SQLSTATE[' error strings. Response body changes in structure between id=1 and id=1' indicating query manipulation.

name: Laravel Route Missing auth Middleware (IDOR / Unauthenticated Access)
description: Laravel API routes in routes/api.php that lack the auth:sanctum or auth middleware group are publicly accessible. Combined with predictable resource IDs, this allows unauthenticated enumeration and retrieval of other users' data.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the app while authenticated to collect all API endpoint URLs. Then replay each request without the Authorization or session cookie. Compare response status codes and body sizes between authenticated and unauthenticated requests.
detection_signal: Unauthenticated request to an API endpoint (e.g., GET /api/users/42, GET /api/orders/7) returns HTTP 200 with a full JSON body rather than HTTP 401 or 403.

name: PHP Object Deserialization via unserialize()
owasp_category: A08:2021 Software and Data Integrity Failures
description: PHP applications that pass user-controlled data to unserialize() are vulnerable to PHP object injection. Attackers supply crafted serialized payloads that trigger magic methods (__wakeup, __destruct) on loaded classes, often achieving remote code execution via POP chains.
runtime_discovery: Inspect cookies, hidden form fields, URL parameters, and API request bodies for base64-encoded or raw PHP serialized strings (starting with 'O:' or 'a:'). Replay the request after injecting a modified serialized object that references a known gadget class and observe for OOB interaction, timing difference, or error disclosure.
detection_signal: Cookie or parameter value matches regex ^[OaCSb]:[0-9]+: (PHP serialize format). Injecting a malformed serialized string returns a distinct error ('unserialize(): Error at offset', 'Class not found') confirming unserialize() code path is reached.

name: Laravel Sanctum / Passport Token Returned in API Response Body
description: Some Laravel API implementations return the plaintext Sanctum personal access token or Passport access token in the login response body and also persist it in localStorage via JavaScript, rather than using HttpOnly cookies. This exposes the token to XSS-based theft.
runtime_discovery: Perform a login POST and inspect the JSON response body for fields named token, access_token, or api_token. Then inspect the browser's localStorage (via JavaScript console or browser tools) for stored token values.
detection_signal: POST /api/login response JSON contains a key access_token, token, or api_token with a value matching Laravel Sanctum token format (a long alphanumeric string or a pipe-delimited id|hash). localStorage contains a key with the same value.

name: Laravel Overly Permissive CORS Configuration
description: Laravel's fruitcake/laravel-cors (now built-in) middleware can be misconfigured with 'allowed_origins' => ['*'] and 'supports_credentials' => true, or with a regex origin reflection. This allows any origin to make credentialed cross-origin requests and read API responses.
runtime_discovery: Send requests to authenticated API endpoints with crafted Origin headers (Origin: https://evil.example.com) and observe the Access-Control-Allow-Origin and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials response headers.
detection_signal: Response includes both 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com' (reflected) and 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true', or 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *' combined with session-cookie-authenticated endpoints returning data.

name: PHP/Laravel Backup and Config Files Accessible
description: Developers often leave editor backup files (.php~, .php.bak, .php.swp), config backups (config.php.bak), or zipped source archives (backup.zip, site.tar.gz) in the web root, exposing PHP source code or configuration with credentials.
runtime_discovery: For every .php file discovered during crawling, probe common backup suffixes: .php~, .php.bak, .php.old, .php.swp, .php.orig. Also probe top-level paths: /backup.zip, /backup.tar.gz, /db.sql, /.git/config, /composer.json, /composer.lock.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response for a backup filename variant (e.g., /index.php.bak) with Content-Type text/plain or application/octet-stream and body containing PHP source tokens ('<?php', 'DB_PASSWORD', '$password'). HTTP 200 for /.git/config with [core] and [remote] git configuration.

name: Insecure Direct Object Reference on Eloquent Auto-Increment IDs
description: Laravel RESTful routes that use sequential integer primary keys (the Eloquent default) without ownership checks allow authenticated users to enumerate and access other users' resources by incrementing the ID parameter.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as user A and note the IDs in their resource URLs (e.g., /api/invoices/42). Switch to user B's session and request /api/invoices/41, /api/invoices/43, etc. Compare response content to confirm whether user B can read user A's data.
detection_signal: Authenticated as user B, GET /api/invoices/42 (owned by user A) returns HTTP 200 with a full resource body rather than HTTP 403 or HTTP 404. Auth-differential comparison confirms different user context returns same data.

name: Unrestricted PHP File Upload Leading to Webshell
description: File upload endpoints that validate only the client-supplied MIME type or file extension (without server-side magic-byte checks or storage outside the web root) can be abused to upload a .php webshell that is then executed by the web server.
runtime_discovery: Submit a file upload form with a file named shell.php (or shell.php.jpg, shell.pHp) containing '<?php system($_GET["cmd"]); ?>'. After upload, navigate to the returned file URL and append ?cmd=id to test for command execution. Also try MIME-type bypass: send Content-Type: image/jpeg with a .php file.
detection_signal: The upload endpoint returns a URL path ending in .php (or .pHp, .php5, .phtml). A GET request to that URL with ?cmd=whoami returns a response body containing a Unix username string (e.g., 'www-data') rather than a download prompt or image.

name: Laravel Session Cookie Missing Secure/HttpOnly/SameSite Flags
description: Laravel session cookies (laravel_session) or CSRF tokens misconfigured without the Secure, HttpOnly, or SameSite=Strict attributes are vulnerable to theft via XSS, network sniffing on HTTP, and CSRF attacks respectively.
runtime_discovery: Perform a standard login and inspect the Set-Cookie response header on the session establishment response. Check each flag: Secure (only sent over HTTPS), HttpOnly (not accessible via JS), and SameSite value (Strict/Lax vs None).
detection_signal: Set-Cookie header for laravel_session (or XSRF-TOKEN) is missing the HttpOnly attribute, missing the Secure attribute when the site is HTTPS, or sets SameSite=None without also setting Secure. Observable via response header inspection without any active payload.

name: ASP.NET Model Binding Mass Assignment (Overposting)
description: ASP.NET model binders automatically map HTTP request fields to C# model properties. If the controller action does not whitelist bindable fields, an attacker can POST extra fields (e.g., IsAdmin=true, Role=Admin, Balance=9999) that overwrite protected database columns.
runtime_discovery: Submit every discovered form with appended hidden fields matching common sensitive property names (IsAdmin, Role, RoleId, IsActive, Balance, Price, ApprovedById, OwnerId). Observe whether the extra fields silently persist in subsequent GET responses, audit-trail entries, or API reads. Auth-diff: compare account state between a normal user who submitted the field and one who did not.
detection_signal: POST with extra parameter (e.g. IsAdmin=true) followed by GET of same resource returns the mutated value, or a 200 OK with no validation error when the field should be server-side-only. A field absent from the rendered HTML form but accepted without a 400/422 response is a positive signal.

name: ASP.NET ViewState MAC Disabled / Weak Machine Key
description: ASP.NET WebForms serializes page state into a Base64-encoded __VIEWSTATE field. If EnableViewStateMac=false or the machineKey is weak/default, an attacker can forge or deserialize the ViewState, leading to arbitrary server-side object deserialization (RCE) or privilege escalation.
runtime_discovery: Crawl all WebForms pages and extract __VIEWSTATE values from hidden inputs. Decode from Base64 and inspect the first bytes for an HMAC suffix. Attempt to submit a tampered ViewState (e.g., flip bits in the payload) and observe whether the server accepts it (200 OK) or rejects it (ViewState validation error / 500).
detection_signal: Tampered __VIEWSTATE accepted (200 OK) with no MAC validation error in the response body. Alternatively, ysoserial.net-generated payloads returning an error that differs from a normal MAC failure (timing or error string change) indicates deserialization execution path was reached.

name: ASP.NET Yellow Screen of Death / Detailed Error Pages Exposed
description: When customErrors mode=Off or the developer exception page is enabled in production, ASP.NET returns full stack traces, SQL query fragments, connection strings, and server-side file paths in the response body on any unhandled exception.
runtime_discovery: Trigger exceptions by fuzzing route parameters with unexpected types (e.g., /product/abc instead of /product/123), submitting malformed JSON/form bodies, and sending requests with invalid Content-Type headers. Observe HTTP 500 responses for stack trace content.
detection_signal: HTTP 500 response body contains strings like 'Stack Trace:', 'Server Error in', 'System.Web.HttpException', '[SqlException', or a full C# namespace path. Also look for the yellow-bordered ASP.NET error page HTML structure or Developer Exception Page HTML in production.

name: ASP.NET Forms Authentication Ticket in Non-Secure Cookie
description: The .ASPXAUTH (or custom-named) Forms Authentication ticket cookie is transmitted without the Secure flag, HttpOnly flag, or with a weak timeout, enabling session hijacking over HTTP or via XSS.
runtime_discovery: After authentication, inspect all Set-Cookie headers for the .ASPXAUTH cookie. Check for absence of Secure attribute, absence of HttpOnly, SameSite=None or missing, and excessively long expiry times. Also probe for the cookie being sent over plain HTTP by making a forced HTTP redirect to the app.
detection_signal: Set-Cookie header for .ASPXAUTH (or .AspNet.ApplicationCookie / .AspNetCore.Cookies) lacks Secure and/or HttpOnly flags. Or the cookie is returned on a plain HTTP (non-TLS) response. Also flag an unusually long Max-Age (e.g., 30+ days) for a session authentication cookie.

name: ASP.NET Open Redirect via ReturnUrl / ReturnURL Parameter
description: ASP.NET login pages commonly redirect to a ?ReturnUrl= or ?returnURL= query parameter after authentication. If the value is not validated against the application's own origin, attackers can redirect victims to phishing sites post-login.
runtime_discovery: Find login forms by crawling for ReturnUrl/ReturnURL/returnUrl query parameters. Submit the login form with ReturnUrl=https://evil.example.com and observe the Location header in the 302 response. Also try ReturnUrl=//evil.example.com and ReturnUrl=/\evil.example.com to bypass path-prefix checks.
detection_signal: HTTP 302 Location header pointing to an external domain (not the app's own origin) after a successful login. Also trigger if the redirect target starts with // or contains a foreign FQDN, even when preceded by /.

name: ASP.NET Anti-Forgery Token Missing (CSRF)
description: State-changing POST endpoints that lack a validated AntiForgeryToken (or the [ValidateAntiForgeryToken] attribute) are vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery, allowing attacker-controlled pages to trigger actions on behalf of authenticated users.
runtime_discovery: Crawl authenticated state-changing forms. For each form, resubmit the POST request with the __RequestVerificationToken field removed or replaced with a random value. Observe whether the server returns 400/403 (token validated) or 200/302 (token ignored).
detection_signal: POST to a state-changing endpoint without a __RequestVerificationToken (or with an arbitrary token value) returns HTTP 200 or 302 and the action takes effect (record created/deleted, settings changed). A correctly protected endpoint should return 400 Bad Request or 403 Forbidden.

name: SQL Injection via Raw Queries in ASP.NET (Entity Framework / ADO.NET)
description: Controllers using ADO.NET SqlCommand with string concatenation, or Entity Framework FromSqlRaw/ExecuteSqlRaw with unsanitized user input, are vulnerable to classic SQL injection enabling data exfiltration, authentication bypass, or data manipulation.
runtime_discovery: Inject SQL metacharacters (', --, ;, 1=1, WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5') into all form fields, query parameters, and route segments that are used for data lookup. Observe for database error strings in the response, unexpected data returns, or response-time increases (time-based blind).
detection_signal: Response contains strings like 'SqlException', 'Unclosed quotation mark', 'Incorrect syntax near', 'System.Data.SqlClient', or 'ORA-00933'. Alternatively, boolean-based: injecting ' OR '1'='1 returns more rows than a baseline. Time-based: WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5' increases response time by ~5 seconds.

name: Insecure Direct Object Reference via Sequential ID in ASP.NET Routes
description: ASP.NET apps commonly expose sequential integer or GUID identifiers in URLs (/api/orders/1042, /api/users/5). Without per-request authorization checks, any authenticated user can access or modify resources owned by other users by incrementing the ID.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as user A and note resource IDs visible in responses. Then with user B's session (auth-diff) or even unauthenticated, request those same IDs. Also enumerate adjacent IDs (+1, -1). Observe whether the server returns 403/404 or the resource content.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response body containing another user's PII, order data, or account content when the requesting session does not own that resource ID. Auth-diff: resource returned to user B that was created by user A, with matching content.

name: ASP.NET Version Disclosure via X-Powered-By / Server Headers
description: Default IIS + ASP.NET installations emit 'X-Powered-By: ASP.NET', 'X-AspNet-Version: 4.0.30319', and 'Server: Microsoft-IIS/10.0' response headers, advertising exact framework and server versions that help attackers narrow exploit selection.
runtime_discovery: Send any HTTP request to the application root and all crawled paths. Inspect response headers for X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version, X-AspNetMvc-Version, and Server header content.
detection_signal: Response headers contain any of: X-Powered-By: ASP.NET, X-AspNet-Version (any value), X-AspNetMvc-Version (any value), or Server: Microsoft-IIS/<version>. Presence of specific version numbers (e.g., 4.0.30319) is a higher-severity signal than a generic framework disclosure.

name: ASP.NET Session Fixation (ASP.NET_SessionId Not Rotated on Auth)
description: If the ASP.NET_SessionId cookie is not regenerated upon successful login, an attacker who can set a victim's session cookie (e.g., via a subdomain XSS or network interception) can pre-plant a known session ID and hijack the authenticated session after the victim logs in.
runtime_discovery: Record the ASP.NET_SessionId value from a pre-authentication (anonymous) request. Submit valid credentials via the login form. Compare the ASP.NET_SessionId in the post-login Set-Cookie (or Cookie echo) with the pre-login value. If they are identical, session fixation is present.
detection_signal: The ASP.NET_SessionId value in the post-authentication response is identical to the pre-authentication session ID. A secure app always issues a new session ID (new Set-Cookie with a different token) immediately after credential verification succeeds.

name: ASP.NET CORS Misconfiguration — Wildcard or Reflected Origin with Credentials
description: ASP.NET Core CORS policies that reflect arbitrary Origin headers or use AllowAnyOrigin combined with AllowCredentials allow attacker-controlled sites to make credentialed cross-origin requests and read protected API responses.
runtime_discovery: Send requests with arbitrary Origin headers (e.g., Origin: https://evil.attacker.com) to all API endpoints. Inspect the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header for reflection of the submitted origin combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true.
detection_signal: Response contains both Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.attacker.com (exact reflection of probe origin) and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. Or Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on endpoints that set authentication cookies. Either combination allows cross-origin session data theft.

name: ASP.NET HTTP Verb Tampering — Authorization Bypass via Method Override
description: Some ASP.NET route configurations or older middleware honor X-HTTP-Method-Override or _method form fields, allowing attackers to disguise a DELETE or PUT as a POST, potentially bypassing verb-based authorization filters that only restrict certain HTTP methods.
runtime_discovery: For protected endpoints (e.g., DELETE /api/resource/1 returns 401/403), retry as POST /api/resource/1 with header X-HTTP-Method-Override: DELETE or form field _method=DELETE. Also try X-HTTP-Method: DELETE and X-METHOD-OVERRIDE: DELETE. Compare response codes and side-effects.
detection_signal: A request that returns 401/403 when sent as DELETE/PUT returns 200 or 204 when sent as POST with a method override header or form field. The side effect (record deleted, resource updated) is also present, confirming bypass rather than a response-code-only difference.

name: ASP.NET Identity Username Enumeration via Differential Login Responses
description: ASP.NET Identity login pages often return different error messages or response times depending on whether the submitted username exists in the database, allowing an attacker to enumerate valid accounts.
runtime_discovery: Submit login requests with a known-valid username + wrong password vs. a clearly nonexistent username + wrong password. Compare response body text, HTTP status codes, and response timing. Also compare password-reset flow responses for the same two cases.
detection_signal: Differential response: body text differs (e.g., 'Invalid password' vs. 'Account not found'), or response time is measurably longer for valid usernames (indicating a bcrypt hash computation was attempted). Also flag if password-reset flow leaks 'If this email exists, we will send...' is absent for invalid emails.

name: ASP.NET Path Traversal via Static File Middleware or Physical File Providers
description: Misconfigured UseStaticFiles middleware or PhysicalFileProvider with an insufficiently restricted root can be exploited with dot-dot-slash sequences to read files outside the intended web root, including appsettings.json, web.config, or .env files.
runtime_discovery: Probe known file paths using traversal sequences: /static/../appsettings.json, /files/..%2F..%2Fweb.config, /assets/%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fappsettings.Production.json. Also try URL-encoded and double-encoded variants. Observe whether the response returns 200 with file content vs. 400/404.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response body containing recognizable configuration file content: JSON with 'ConnectionStrings', 'Logging', or 'JwtSecret' keys (appsettings.json pattern), or XML containing '<connectionStrings>' or '<appSettings>' (web.config pattern). A 404 that suddenly becomes 200 on traversal is the key signal.

name: Missing Security Headers on ASP.NET Responses (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)
description: ASP.NET applications without explicit security header middleware (e.g., NWebSec, custom middleware, or UseHsts/UseXContentTypeOptions) omit Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, and Referrer-Policy headers, increasing exposure to clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and protocol downgrade attacks.
runtime_discovery: Crawl authenticated and unauthenticated pages. For each response, inspect headers for presence of: Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options (or CSP frame-ancestors), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy. Score absence of each.
detection_signal: One or more critical security headers absent from HTTP responses: no Content-Security-Policy on pages rendering user-controlled HTML, no Strict-Transport-Security on HTTPS endpoints, no X-Frame-Options (enabling clickjacking), or no X-Content-Type-Options. Multiple absences in combination elevate severity.

name: Missing or Misconfigured CORS in Go HTTP Handlers
description: Go's net/http default mux and popular routers (gin, echo) ship with no CORS headers unless the developer explicitly adds them. A common misconfiguration is setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin to the request's Origin header verbatim, or to '*' while also allowing credentials, enabling cross-origin data theft.
runtime_discovery: Send a cross-origin preflight OPTIONS request with an arbitrary Origin header (e.g., Origin: https://attacker.example). Observe the response for reflected origin or wildcard in Access-Control-Allow-Origin combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true.
detection_signal: Response contains Access-Control-Allow-Origin equal to the attacker-supplied Origin value AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true; or Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on an authenticated endpoint (credentialed cookie present in session).

name: net/http pprof Debug Endpoints Exposed on Default Mux
description: Importing net/http/pprof registers /debug/pprof/* routes on the default ServeMux as a side effect. If the server uses the default mux (http.ListenAndServe with nil handler) and the pprof import is present anywhere in the binary, goroutine dumps, heap profiles, and CPU traces are publicly accessible—leaking stack traces, memory contents, and internal paths.
runtime_discovery: Crawl /debug/pprof/, /debug/pprof/goroutine, /debug/pprof/heap, /debug/pprof/cmdline without authentication. Check for HTTP 200 with profiling data.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response body at /debug/pprof/ containing 'goroutine', 'heap', 'allocs', or 'cmdline' index links; or /debug/pprof/goroutine?debug=1 returning a live goroutine stack dump.

name: Go Runtime / Database Error Messages Leaked in HTTP Responses
description: Go applications frequently pass raw error values (database/sql, gorm, pgx, os.Open) directly into http.Error or JSON responses. This exposes internal SQL queries, table names, file paths, driver strings, and connection strings to the client in plain text.
runtime_discovery: Trigger validation errors by submitting malformed input, invalid IDs, or non-existent resources. Observe 4xx/5xx response bodies for Go-specific error strings and internal details.
detection_signal: Response body contains strings matching Go error patterns: 'sql:', 'pq:', 'no rows in result', 'dial tcp', 'connection refused', absolute Unix file paths (/home/*, /var/*, /go/src/), or goroutine stack trace markers like 'goroutine \d+ [running]'.

name: Gin/Echo Route Groups Missing Authentication Middleware
description: In gin and echo, authentication middleware is applied per route group. Developers frequently add a new route to the wrong group (the unauthenticated group) or forget to apply the middleware to a group, leaving sensitive endpoints accessible without a token or session cookie.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the application with two sessions: authenticated and unauthenticated. Compare the HTTP status codes and response body sizes for all discovered endpoints. Any endpoint returning 200 with substantial data for the unauthenticated session is a candidate.
detection_signal: Unauthenticated request to an API endpoint returns HTTP 200 with a non-empty JSON body identical or similar in size/structure to the authenticated response, where the endpoint is not explicitly public (e.g., /api/v1/admin/*, /api/v1/users/*).

name: HTTP Host Header Injection (Password Reset / Redirect Poisoning)
description: Go's net/http gives handlers full access to r.Host. Applications that use r.Host to construct password-reset URLs, redirect targets, or canonical links without validation allow an attacker to inject an arbitrary hostname, redirecting victims to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
runtime_discovery: Submit password-reset or email-confirmation requests with a modified Host header (Host: attacker.example). Observe whether the link in the email or the Location response header reflects the attacker-supplied host.
detection_signal: The Location response header or response body URL contains the injected Host value verbatim. Alternatively, a redirect response (3xx) target host matches the probe value rather than the application's canonical domain.

name: Open Redirect via Unvalidated URL Parameter
description: Go handlers that call http.Redirect with a URL taken directly from a query parameter (e.g., ?next=, ?redirect=, ?return_to=) without validating that the destination is within the same origin can be abused for phishing and OAuth token harvesting.
runtime_discovery: Fuzz common redirect parameters (?next=, ?redirect_uri=, ?return=, ?url=, ?dest=) with an external URL (https://attacker.example). Follow the redirect chain and observe the final destination.
detection_signal: HTTP 3xx response where the Location header value matches or starts with the injected external URL, or a redirect chain that ultimately lands on the attacker-supplied domain.

name: Missing Security Response Headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)
description: Go's net/http and the gin/echo frameworks write no security headers by default. Applications that do not apply a security-headers middleware (or equivalent) are missing Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, and Referrer-Policy, enabling clickjacking, MIME-sniffing, and mixed-content attacks.
runtime_discovery: Issue a GET request to the application root and any rendered HTML pages. Inspect all response headers for the presence of security-relevant headers.
detection_signal: Response is missing two or more of: Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options (or CSP frame-ancestors), Referrer-Policy. Absence confirmed by header enumeration on 200 responses.

name: Insecure Direct Object Reference via Sequential Numeric ID
description: Go REST APIs built with gin or echo commonly use integer route parameters (/api/v1/users/:id, /api/v1/orders/:id). Without server-side ownership checks, an authenticated user can increment or decrement the ID to access resources belonging to other users.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as user A, obtain a resource ID from a normal workflow, then enumerate adjacent IDs (id-1, id-2, id+1) as user B (or unauthenticated). Compare response body ownership fields.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response for an ID outside the authenticated user's known resource set, where the response body contains user-identifying fields (email, user_id, name) differing from the requesting user's identity.

name: JWT 'none' Algorithm Accepted or HS256/RS256 Confusion
description: Go JWT libraries (dgrijalva/jwt-go, golang-jwt/jwt) have historically required explicit algorithm allowlisting. Applications that accept any algorithm in the header allow an attacker to forge tokens using the 'none' algorithm or to switch RS256 to HS256 and sign with the public key.
runtime_discovery: Obtain a valid JWT, modify the header to alg:none and strip the signature, then replay the token to authenticated endpoints. Separately, attempt alg:HS256 with the server's public key as the HMAC secret.
detection_signal: Authenticated endpoint returns HTTP 200 (or same data as a valid token) when presented with a JWT bearing alg:none and no signature, or alg:HS256 signed with the public RSA key bytes.

name: Server-Side Request Forgery via URL Parameter in Go HTTP Client
description: Go services that accept a URL parameter and pass it to http.Get or http.Client.Do without restricting the scheme or host are vulnerable to SSRF. Go's http.Client will follow redirects by default and supports file://, gopher://, and http:// to internal hosts including cloud metadata services.
runtime_discovery: Submit URL parameters pointing to internal addresses: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ (AWS), http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/, http://127.0.0.1:<common port>. Observe whether the response body contains metadata content or internal service responses.
detection_signal: Response body contains cloud metadata markers ('ami-id', 'instance-id', 'computeMetadata', 'iam/security-credentials') or internal service banners; or response timing/size differs significantly from an invalid host, indicating a live internal connection.

name: Path Traversal in Static File Serving (gin Static / echo Static)
